topics.title: Introduction to FEM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET723 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to FEM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Finite Element Method (FEM)** is a powerful, systematic, and general-purpose numerical technique used to obtain approximate solutions to a wide variety of engineering problems governed by differential equations. It discretizes a complex continuous domain (the structure or body) into a finite number of smaller, simpler, and well-defined sub-regions called **finite elements**. The unknown field variable (e.g., displacement, temperature, pressure) within each element is approximated by simple interpolation functions (shape functions) defined in terms of the nodal values. By assembling the governing equations of all elements and applying the boundary conditions, a global system of algebraic equations is obtained, which is then solved to predict the physical behavior of the entire domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** FEM is classified as a *variational* or *weighted residual* numerical method, distinguishing it from the Finite Difference Method (FDM), which is based on direct differential operator discretization. The core identity of FEM is the *piecewise polynomial approximation over non-overlapping subdomains*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition

Imagine you want to estimate the **irregular area** of a curved plot of land. You cannot compute it with a single standard geometric formula (like $\pi r^2$ or $L \times B$). The practical, intuitive engineering approach is to:

1. Throw a grid of squares (or triangles) over the plot — this is **discretization**.
2. Count how many whole and partial shapes are inside the boundary — these are your **finite elements**.
3. Calculate the area of each small shape using simple formulas — these are your **shape function integrations**.
4. Add them all up to approximate the total area — this is the **assembly and global solution**.

> FEM follows the exact same philosophy for **stress, strain, heat flow, fluid flow, and electromagnetics**. Instead of guessing the entire complex behavior, we guess the behavior inside small, manageable pieces (elements) and stitch them together to approximate the global behavior.

### 1.3 Physical & Numerical Constants Used in FEM

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent displacement (or field) components required to define the configuration of a node or element. In 2D plane stress, it is typically **2** ($u, v$); in 3D, it is **3** ($u, v, w$).
- **Strain Displacement Matrix $[B]$:** Dimensionless operator that relates strains to nodal displacements.
- **Element Stiffness Matrix $[k]$:** Units of **N/m** (Force/Length).
- **Global Stiffness Matrix $[K]$:** A large, sparse, symmetric, banded, and often positive-definite matrix representing the assembled structure.
- **Shape Functions $[N]$:** Dimensionless interpolation polynomials satisfying the **Kronecker Delta property**: $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$.
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Dimensionless material constant, typically $0 < \nu < 0.5$.
- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Units of **N/m²** or **Pa**.

> [!NOTE]
> The accuracy of FEM converges toward the exact solution as the number of elements $n \to \infty$ and the element size $h \to 0$. This convergence is mathematically guaranteed for well-posed problems in the energy norm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why FEM is Needed: Limitations of Classical Analytical Methods

Classical methods (closed-form solutions using separation of variables, eigenfunction expansions, etc.) suffer from severe practical limitations:

- **Geometric Rigidity:** They only work for regular shapes (rectangles, circles, simple cylinders).
- **Boundary Complexity:** They cannot handle arbitrary loadings, mixed boundary conditions, or complex material property distributions.
- **Material Heterogeneity:** Closed-form solutions assume homogeneous, isotropic, and linear materials.

FEM bypasses all these restrictions, providing a unified framework for **arbitrary geometry, arbitrary loading, heterogeneous materials, and non-linear behavior**.

### 2.2 General Steps / Workflow of FEM (The 3-Stage Paradigm)

The complete FEM analysis is universally divided into three logical stages, frequently tested in KTU examinations:

**Stage I — Pre-Processing (The largest, most critical stage)**

- **Step 1:** Discretize the domain into elements (1D lines, 2D triangles/quadrilaterals, 3D tetrahedra/hexahedra).
- **Step 2:** Number the nodes and elements (this defines the mesh connectivity).
- **Step 3:** Assume a shape function $[N]$ to approximate the field variable $u(x)$ over each element.
- **Step 4:** Derive the **element matrices** (stiffness $[k]$, mass $[m]$, conductivity $[c]$, etc.) and **element load vectors** $\{f\}^e$.

**Stage II — Solution (Computational stage)**

- **Step 5:** Assemble the element matrices into the global system:

$$\left[K\right] \left\{U\right\} = \left\{F\right\}$$

- **Step 6:** Impose the **essential (Dirichlet) boundary conditions** and modify the system accordingly.
- **Step 7:** Solve the resulting linear/non-linear algebraic system using direct (Gauss elimination) or iterative (Conjugate Gradient) solvers.

**Stage III — Post-Processing (Result extraction stage)**

- **Step 8:** Compute the **derived quantities** — strains ($\varepsilon$), stresses ($\sigma$), temperature gradients, heat fluxes, reaction forces, etc.
- **Step 9:** Visualize the results using **contour plots, deformed shape plots, vector plots, and animations**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often pose a 7-mark question asking to "Explain the general procedure of FEM with a neat block diagram." Memorize the 3-stage paradigm above for guaranteed marks.

### 2.3 Mathematical Foundation: The Weighted Residual & Weak Form

The governing differential equation (say, a 1D axial bar) is the **strong form**:

$$-\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) + f(x) = 0, \quad x \in (0, L)$$

with boundary conditions $u(0) = 0$ (essential) and $EA \frac{du}{dx}\Big\vert_{x=L} = P$ (natural).

Because exact solutions are hard, FEM assumes an approximate solution $u^h(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) u_i$ and enforces a **weighted integral statement** (Galerkin's method) to derive a system of algebraic equations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Term** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Approximate Solution | $u^h(x) = \sum N_i(x) \, u_i$ | Piecewise polynomial field | m (or field unit) |
| Shape Function Property | $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ | Kronecker Delta (interpolation) | Dimensionless |
| Strain-Displacement | $\{\varepsilon\} = [B] \{d\}^e$ | Kinematics of deformation | Dimensionless |
| Constitutive Law | $\{\sigma\} = [D] \{\varepsilon\}$ | Hooke's Law (material law) | N/m² (Pa) |
| Element Stiffness | $[k]^e = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV$ | Energy stored in element | N/m |
| Element Load Vector | $\{f\}^e = \int_{V_e} [N]^T \{b\} \, dV + \int_{S_e} [N]^T \{t\} \, dS$ | Body forces + Tractions | N |
| Global Assembly | $[K] = \bigotimes_e [k]^e, \quad \{F\} = \bigotimes_e \{f\}^e$ | Sum over all elements | N/m, N |
| Equilibrium | $[K] \{U\} = \{F\}$ | Global equilibrium equation | Algebraic |
| Strain Energy | $U = \frac{1}{2} \{U\}^T [K] \{U\}$ | Total potential energy | N·m (Joules) |
| Convergence Rate | $\Vert e \Vert_{L_2} = O(h^{p+1})$ | L2 norm error | m |
| Spectral Condition | $\lambda_{\min} \leq \lambda \leq \lambda_{\max}$ | Eigenvalue bounds of $[K]$ | N/m |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, the notation $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ uses the standard Kronecker delta. Avoid using the vertical pipe `|` for absolute values in your answer sheets to maintain a clean, exam-friendly look — use parentheses or `\vert`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

FEM is the **backbone of modern Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE)**:

- **Aerospace:** Wing flutter analysis, composite layup optimization for Boeing/Airbus.
- **Automotive:** Crashworthiness simulation, NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) analysis.
- **Civil:** Skyscraper seismic analysis, dam stress analysis, bridge load rating.
- **Biomedical:** Stent design, bone implant stress shielding, blood flow modeling.
- **Electronics:** Chip thermal management, PCB warpage analysis.
- **Manufacturing:** Metal forging, sheet metal forming, injection mold filling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Strong Form to Weak Form (Galerkin Approach)

Consider a 1D bar of varying cross-section under axial loading. The governing **strong form** (equilibrium equation) is:

$$-\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA(x)\frac{du}{dx}\right) = f(x), \quad 0 < x < L$$

The associated boundary conditions are:

$$u(0) = 0 \quad \text{(Essential / Dirichlet)}$$

$$\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right)_{x=L} = P \quad \text{(Natural / Neumann)}$$

**Step 1: Define the trial (approximate) solution**

We seek an approximate solution from a finite-dimensional subspace $S^h$:

$$u^h(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) \, u_i = [N(x)] \{u\}$$

**Step 2: Define the test (weighting) function**

The weighting functions $W_j(x)$ must satisfy the homogeneous form of the essential boundary conditions, i.e., $W_j(0) = 0$. In **Galerkin's method**, we choose the test functions identical to the trial shape functions: $W_j(x) = N_j(x)$.

**Step 3: Form the Weighted Residual Statement**

Multiply the differential equation by $W_j$ and integrate over the domain $[0, L]$:

$$\int_0^L W_j \left[ -\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du^h}{dx}\right) - f(x) \right] dx = 0$$

**Step 4: Integration by Parts (the critical step that generates the "weak form")**

Apply integration by parts to the second-order derivative term to reduce the order of differentiation (this is the heart of the FEM formulation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^L W_j \left[-\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du^h}{dx}\right)\right] dx &= \left[-W_j \, EA\frac{du^h}{dx}\right]_0^L + \int_0^L \frac{dW_j}{dx} EA\frac{du^h}{dx} \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the boundary conditions**

Since $W_j(0) = 0$ (from the essential BC), the lower limit term vanishes. At $x = L$, we substitute the natural BC $EA \frac{du^h}{dx}\Big\vert_L = P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[-W_j \, EA\frac{du^h}{dx}\right]_0^L &= -W_j(L) \cdot P - 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Assemble the complete weak form**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^L \frac{dW_j}{dx} \, EA \, \frac{du^h}{dx} \, dx - \int_0^L W_j \, f(x) \, dx - W_j(L) P = 0, \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Substitute the trial and test functions**

Substitute $u^h = \sum N_i u_i$ and $W_j = N_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i \int_0^L \frac{dN_j}{dx} \, EA \, \frac{dN_i}{dx} \, dx = \int_0^L N_j \, f(x) \, dx + N_j(L) \, P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Recognize the matrix form**

Define the element stiffness matrix and load vector:

$$k_{ji} = \int_0^L \frac{dN_j}{dx} \, EA \, \frac{dN_i}{dx} \, dx, \quad f_j = \int_0^L N_j \, f(x) \, dx + N_j(L) P$$

The system in matrix form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[k] \{u\} = \{f\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This elegant derivation shows how a 2nd-order ODE is reduced to a system of linear algebraic equations, suitable for digital computer solution.

### 3.2 Derivation: Element Stiffness Matrix for a 2-Node Linear Bar Element

For a uniform bar element of length $L_e$, constant $EA$, and no distributed load:

**Step 1: Linear shape functions**

For nodes 1 and 2 with local coordinate $x \in [0, L_e]$:

$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L_e}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L_e}$$

**Step 2: Compute derivatives**

$$\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L_e}, \quad \frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{1}{L_e}$$

**Step 3: Form the strain-displacement matrix**

$$[B] = \frac{1}{L_e} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute each entry of the stiffness matrix**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{11} &= \int_0^{L_e} \left(-\frac{1}{L_e}\right) (EA) \left(-\frac{1}{L_e}\right) dx = \frac{EA}{L_e} \\
k_{12} &= \int_0^{L_e} \left(-\frac{1}{L_e}\right) (EA) \left(\frac{1}{L_e}\right) dx = -\frac{EA}{L_e} \\
k_{21} &= -\frac{EA}{L_e}, \quad k_{22} = \frac{EA}{L_e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final assembled element matrix**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[k]^e = \frac{EA}{L_e} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This $2 \times 2$ symmetric, singular (before applying BCs) matrix is the foundation for assembling any 1D bar structure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: The FEM Computational Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete data-flow and decision logic between the pre-processing, solution, and post-processing stages, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition Geometry Material Loads BCs] --> B[Discretization Domain into Elements]
    B --> C[Node and Element Numbering Mesh Generation]
    C --> D[Select Shape Functions N and Order]
    D --> E[Derive Element Matrices k and f]
    E --> F[Assembly into Global System K and F]
    F --> G{Apply Essential Boundary Conditions}
    G --> H[Solve Linear or Nonlinear Algebraic System K U = F]
    H --> I[Compute Derived Quantities Strain Stress Flux]
    I --> J[Post-Processing Contour Plots Deformed Shape]
    J --> K[Validation and Convergence Check]
    K --> L{Mesh Refinement Needed?}
    L -- Yes --> B
    L -- No --> M[Final Engineering Decision]
```

**Caption:** The adaptive feedback loop ensures $h$-convergence (mesh refinement) or $p$-convergence (polynomial order enrichment) until the solution stabilizes within an acceptable error tolerance.

### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic: Domain Discretization Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Continuous Complex Domain Omega] -->|Discretization| B[Mesh of Finite Elements]
    B --> C1[Element 1 Triangular]
    B --> C2[Element 2 Quadrilateral]
    B --> C3[Element 3 Triangular]
    B --> C4[Element 4 Quadrilateral]
    C1 --> D1[Nodes 1 2 5]
    C2 --> D2[Nodes 2 3 5]
    C3 --> D3[Nodes 1 5 4]
    C4 --> D4[Nodes 3 6 5]
    D1 --> E[Shared Nodal DOF ensures Continuity]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Global Continuous Approximation]
```

**Caption:** The critical property of FEM is **$C^0$ continuity** (continuity of the field variable, not its derivative) across element boundaries when using standard Lagrange shape functions. This is enforced by sharing the same nodal DOF between adjacent elements.

### 4.3 Comparison Block: FEM vs. Other Numerical Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Numerical Methods for PDEs] --> B[Finite Difference Method FDM]
    A --> C[Finite Volume Method FVM]
    A --> D[Finite Element Method FEM]
    A --> E[Boundary Element Method BEM]
    B --> B1[Strong Form Direct]
    C --> C1[Conservation Laws]
    D --> D1[Weak Form Variational]
    E --> E1[Boundary Integral]
    D1 --> D2[Best for Complex Geometry and Heterogeneous Materials]
```

**Caption:** KTU examiners frequently test the comparison table between FEM and FDM. The key takeaway is that **FEM uses the weak (integral) form**, making it vastly superior for irregular geometries and complex boundary conditions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the Finite Element Method. List any four advantages of FEM over classical methods.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Finite Element Method (FEM)** is a numerical procedure for obtaining approximate solutions to boundary value problems governed by differential equations. It divides the domain into a finite number of small sub-domains (elements), approximates the unknown field variable in each element by simple polynomial interpolation functions (shape functions), and assembles the element equations into a global system $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ for solution.

**Four advantages over classical methods:**

1. **Handles complex geometries** — irregular boundaries pose no difficulty.
2. **Handles heterogeneous and anisotropic materials** — material properties can vary element-wise.
3. **Handles arbitrary boundary conditions** and mixed loading scenarios seamlessly.
4. **Provides piecewise continuous solutions** with controllable accuracy via mesh refinement.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for valid advantages)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is a shape function? State the two essential properties it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**
A **shape function** $N_i(x)$ is a polynomial interpolation function that defines the approximate value of the field variable at any point inside a finite element as a weighted sum of the nodal values.

$$u^h(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) \, u_i$$

**Two essential properties:**

1. **Kronecker Delta Property:** $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i = j$ and $0$ otherwise. This ensures the approximation recovers the exact nodal value.
2. **Partition of Unity:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) = 1$ for all $x$ in the element. This ensures a constant field (like a rigid body translation) is reproduced exactly.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for properties)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (Common Loss: 2–3 marks):** Students often confuse the *strong form* with the *weak form*. Always state the operator order reduction explicitly when deriving the weak form.
> - **Pitfall 2 (Common Loss: 1–2 marks):** Writing $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ without specifying that it is a *global* system assembled from *element* matrices loses you marks. Be precise: $[K] = \bigotimes_e [k]^e$.
> - **Pitfall 3 (Common Loss: 1 mark):** Forgetting to mention units of $[K]$ (N/m) and $\{F\}$ (N) in numerical problems.
> - **Pitfall 4 (Common Loss: 2 marks):** Not drawing a proper block diagram for the FEM procedure — examiners reserve 2 marks specifically for the visual representation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Derive the weak form (variational form) of the following 1D boundary value problem using the Galerkin weighted residual method:

$$-\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) + cu = f(x), \quad 0 < x < L$$

with boundary conditions $u(0) = 0$ and $EA\frac{du}{dx}\Big\vert_{x=L} = P$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a two-node linear bar element, derive the element stiffness matrix and consistent load vector for a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length. Show all integrations step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Weak Form Derivation:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the trial solution $u^h(x) = \sum N_i(x) u_i$ and weight functions $W_j = N_j$ (Galerkin choice, satisfying $W_j(0)=0$).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Form the weighted residual:

$$\int_0^L N_j \left[ -\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du^h}{dx}\right) + cu^h - f(x) \right] dx = 0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply integration by parts on the highest order derivative:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^L \frac{dN_j}{dx} \, EA \, \frac{du^h}{dx} \, dx + \int_0^L N_j \, c \, u^h \, dx = \int_0^L N_j \, f(x) \, dx + N_j(L) \, P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute the trial and test functions to obtain the algebraic system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i \left[ \int_0^L \frac{dN_j}{dx} EA \frac{dN_i}{dx} dx + \int_0^L c N_j N_i dx \right] = \int_0^L N_j f(x) dx + N_j(L) P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Element Stiffness and Load Vector:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a 2-node bar element of length $L_e$, $N_1 = 1 - \frac{x}{L_e}$ and $N_2 = \frac{x}{L_e}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Strain-displacement matrix:

$$[B] = \frac{1}{L_e}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Stiffness matrix entries:

$$[k]^e = \int_0^{L_e} [B]^T EA [B] \, dx = \frac{EA}{L_e} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (3 marks):** Consistent load vector for UDL $w$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{f\}^e &= \int_0^{L_e} [N]^T w \, dx = \int_0^{L_e} \begin{bmatrix} 1 - \frac{x}{L_e} \\ \frac{x}{L_e} \end{bmatrix} w \, dx \\
&= w \begin{bmatrix} L_e - \frac{L_e}{2} \\ \frac{L_e}{2} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{w L_e}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the three main stages of the Finite Element Method (pre-processing, solution, post-processing) with a neat block diagram. List the key activities in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stepped steel bar consists of two segments in series. Segment 1 has length $L_1 = 1$ m, area $A_1 = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m². Segment 2 has length $L_2 = 0.5$ m, area $A_2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m². Young's modulus $E = 200 \times 10^9$ N/m². The bar is fixed at the left end and subjected to an axial force $P = 50$ kN at the right end. Using 2-node linear bar elements, determine the nodal displacements and the element stresses. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 3 Stages of FEM (Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for full marks):**

| Stage | Key Activities | Marks Allocated |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pre-Processing** | Mesh generation, node numbering, material assignment, shape function selection, derivation of element matrices | 3 |
| **Solution** | Assembly of global $[K]$ and $\{F\}$, application of BCs, solving $[K]\{U\}=\{F\}$ | 2 |
| **Post-Processing** | Strain/stress calculation, reaction force computation, visualization, convergence check | 2 |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Element stiffness matrices.

$$
\begin{aligned}
[k_1] &= \frac{E A_1}{L_1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= 40 \times 10^6 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
[k_2] &= \frac{E A_2}{L_2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= 40 \times 10^6 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Global stiffness matrix assembly (3 nodes, 2 elements):

$$[K] = 40 \times 10^6 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Load vector: $\{F\} = \{0, 0, 50000\}^T$ N (since $P = 50$ kN at node 3).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply BC $u_1 = 0$. Reduce the system to 2 unknowns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
40 \times 10^6 \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 50000 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Solve by matrix inversion.

Determinant: $(2)(1) - (-1)(-1) = 1$. Inverse:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} &= \frac{1}{40 \times 10^6} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 50000 \end{Bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{40 \times 10^6} \begin{Bmatrix} 50000 \\ 100000 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \\ 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \end{Bmatrix} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $u_2 = 1.25$ mm and $u_3 = 2.5$ mm.

**Step 6 (Bonus check):** Element stresses:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{E(u_2 - u_1)}{L_1} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{E(u_3 - u_2)}{L_2} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5} = 500 \text{ MPa}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Introduction to FEM":**

- **FEM** is a *variational / weighted residual* numerical method based on the **weak (integral) form** of the governing differential equation.
- The **three stages** are: **Pre-processing → Solution → Post-processing** (memorize this for guaranteed 7-mark answers).
- The fundamental equation is the **global equilibrium**: $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$, where $[K]$ is symmetric, positive-definite, sparse, and banded.
- **Shape functions** must satisfy: **Kronecker Delta property** and **Partition of Unity**.
- The element stiffness matrix for a 2-node linear bar element is: $[k]^e = \frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- The consistent load vector for a uniformly distributed load $w$ on a 2-node element is: $\{f\}^e = \frac{wL}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$.
- **Strong form** = differential equation; **Weak form** = integral form obtained after integration by parts (reduces the order of differentiation by one).
- **Galerkin's method** chooses the test function = shape function: $W_j(x) = N_j(x)$.
- **Convergence:** The FEM solution converges to the exact solution as the element size $h \to 0$ (h-convergence) or the polynomial order $p \to \infty$ (p-convergence).
- **FEM vs. FDM:** FEM uses the weak form and is geometry-flexible; FDM uses the strong form and requires regular grids.
- **Key advantages:** handles complex geometry, heterogeneous materials, arbitrary BCs, and provides reliable error estimation.
- **Key limitations:** requires significant computational resources, large data storage for 3D problems, and demands expertise in mesh generation and result interpretation.
- **Units reminder:** $[K]$ → N/m, $\{F\}$ → N, $[B]$ → 1/m, $[D]$ → N/m² (Pa), $[N]$ → dimensionless.
- **Forces at fixed nodes** = **Reactions**, which are computed in post-processing as $R = [K_{bb}]\{U_b\} - \{F_b\}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Modelling of Field Problems in Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET723 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Modelling of Field Problems in Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Modelling of Field Problems** is the systematic process of translating a physical phenomenon occurring over a continuous spatial/temporal domain into a precise **Boundary Value Problem (BVP)** or **Initial Value Problem (IVP)** described by **Governing Differential Equations (PDEs)**, along with the associated **domain geometry**, **material properties**, and **boundary/initial conditions**.

A **Field Problem** in engineering is any physical situation where one or more dependent variables (called *field variables*) vary continuously with respect to spatial coordinates and/or time within a defined **domain** $\Omega$. The objective of mathematical modelling is to derive a *well-posed* mathematical statement whose solution approximates (or exactly yields) the physical behaviour of the system.

### General Mathematical Statement of a Field Problem

For a scalar field variable $u(\mathbf{x}, t)$ over a domain $\Omega$ with boundary $\Gamma$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(u) = f \quad \text{in} \ \Omega
$$

$$
\mathcal{B}(u) = g \quad \text{on} \ \Gamma
$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is the **governing differential operator**, $f$ is the **source term**, $\mathcal{B}$ is the **boundary operator**, and $g$ is the **prescribed boundary data**.

## Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Mapping a Mountain's Temperature"**
> Imagine you are standing at the foot of a mountain and wish to know the temperature at *every point* on the mountain at a particular time. You cannot place a thermometer at each point — there are infinitely many. So instead, you:
> 1. Identify the governing physical law (Fourier's law of heat conduction),
> 2. Define the region of interest (the mountain volume $\Omega$),
> 3. Specify the temperature at the base and the heat flux at the top (boundary conditions),
> 4. Specify the initial temperature distribution (initial condition, if transient).
>
> The resulting **Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** is your *mathematical model*. Solving it gives you the temperature field $T(\mathbf{x}, t)$ everywhere — this is the essence of mathematical modelling of a field problem.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Thermal conductivity:** $k$ with units $\mathrm{W/(m \cdot K)}$
- **Density:** $\rho$ with units $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$
- **Specific heat:** $c_p$ with units $\mathrm{J/(kg \cdot K)}$
- **Young's Modulus:** $E$ with units $\mathrm{Pa}$ or $\mathrm{N/m^2}$
- **Dynamic viscosity:** $\mu$ with units $\mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \mathrm{F/m}$
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \mathrm{H/m}$

### Examples of Engineering Field Problems

| **Field Problem** | **Field Variable** | **Governing Equation Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Heat conduction in a solid | Temperature $T(\mathbf{x},t)$ | Elliptic (steady) / Parabolic (transient) |
| Torsion of a shaft | Prandtl stress function $\phi$ | Poisson equation |
| Fluid flow in a duct | Stream function $\psi$ | Laplace equation |
| Bar under axial load | Displacement $u(x)$ | ODE (boundary value) |
| Seepage through porous media | Pore pressure $p$ | Laplace equation |
| Electrostatic potential | Potential $V$ | Poisson equation |
| Magnetostatic field | Vector potential $\mathbf{A}$ | Poisson vector equation |
| Structural displacement | Displacement $\mathbf{u}$ | Linear elasticity system |

> [!NOTE]
> A **well-posed problem** must satisfy three conditions: (1) *existence* of a solution, (2) *uniqueness* of the solution, and (3) *continuous dependence* of the solution on the data. Hadamard (1902) established this as a prerequisite for any meaningful physical model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The General Procedure of Mathematical Modelling

Mathematical modelling of a field problem is executed through a rigorous sequence of steps. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate these steps clearly:

1. **Problem Identification & Idealization:** Distil the real engineering situation into a tractable idealised form (e.g., neglect radiation in a low-temperature heat transfer problem).
2. **Definition of the Domain $\Omega$:** Specify the geometric region and the boundary $\Gamma = \partial \Omega$ enclosing it.
3. **Selection of the Field Variable(s):** Choose the dependent variable(s) that completely describe the physical state (e.g., $T$, $\mathbf{u}$, $\phi$).
4. **Formulation of the Governing Equation:** Apply the relevant physical conservation law (energy, momentum, mass, charge) to an elemental control volume, leading to a PDE.
5. **Statement of Boundary Conditions:** Specify the value or flux of the field variable on $\Gamma$.
6. **Statement of Initial Conditions (Transient problems only):** Specify the field distribution at $t = 0$.
7. **Solution Strategy Selection:** Choose analytical or numerical (FEM, FDM, FVM) techniques.

## Classification of Field Problems

### (a) Boundary Value Problem (BVP)
- Independent variable: **space only** (steady-state).
- Requires specification of conditions on the *entire* boundary $\Gamma$.
- Example: Steady-state heat conduction, torsion, irrotational flow.

### (b) Initial Value Problem (IVP)
- Independent variable: **time only** (or time + space with prescribed initial spatial distribution).
- Example: Time-dependent ODE in a single coordinate.

### (c) Initial-Boundary Value Problem (IBVP)
- Independent variables: **space and time** (transient field problems).
- Requires both initial conditions on $\Omega$ and boundary conditions on $\Gamma$.
- Example: Transient heat conduction, wave propagation.

## Governing Equations for Common Engineering Field Problems

### 1. Heat Conduction in a Solid (Transient)

Using energy conservation on an elemental control volume:

$$
\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + Q(\mathbf{x}, t)
$$

For **isotropic, homogeneous** material with constant $k$:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T + \frac{Q}{\rho c_p}
$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ is the **thermal diffusivity** ($\mathrm{m^2/s}$).

- **Steady-state** ($\partial T/\partial t = 0$): $\nabla^2 T + \dfrac{Q}{k} = 0$ (Poisson equation)
- **No source** ($Q = 0$): $\nabla^2 T = 0$ (Laplace equation)

### 2. Torsion of a Prismatic Shaft

The **Prandtl stress function** $\phi(x,y)$ satisfies the Poisson equation:

$$
\nabla^2 \phi = -2 G \theta
$$

where $G$ is the shear modulus and $\theta$ is the angle of twist per unit length. On the boundary, $\phi = 0$.

### 3. Fluid Flow — Stream Function Formulation

For incompressible, irrotational 2D flow, the stream function $\psi$ satisfies:

$$
\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega
$$

where $\omega$ is the vorticity. For irrotational flow ($\omega = 0$), this reduces to $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$.

### 4. Linear Elasticity (Solid Mechanics)

Equilibrium equations (in absence of body forces):

$$
\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Constitutive relation: $\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \mathbf{D} \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ (Hooke's law)

Kinematic relation: $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\nabla \mathbf{u} + \nabla \mathbf{u}^T\right)$

where $\mathbf{u}$ is the displacement field, $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is the stress tensor, $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ is the strain tensor, and $\mathbf{D}$ is the elasticity matrix.

### 5. Electrostatics

For the electric potential $V$ in a charge-free region:

$$
\nabla^2 V = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace)}
$$

With volume charge density $\rho_e$:

$$
\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho_e}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{(Poisson)}
$$

## Types of Boundary Conditions — The Big Three

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** Boundary conditions must be classified as **Essential (Dirichlet)**, **Natural (Neumann)**, or **Mixed (Robin/Cauchy)**. Each carries different mathematical and physical implications for FEM formulation.

| **Type** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning** | **FEM Terminology** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dirichlet (Essential)** | $u = \bar{u}$ on $\Gamma_u$ | Field variable prescribed | Strongly enforced; modifies $\mathbf{K}$ |
| **Neumann (Natural)** | $q = -k \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = \bar{q}$ on $\Gamma_q$ | Flux prescribed | Naturally satisfied; modifies $\mathbf{F}$ |
| **Robin / Cauchy (Mixed)** | $k \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} + h(u - u_\infty) = 0$ on $\Gamma_m$ | Convection / mixed | Both $\mathbf{K}$ and $\mathbf{F}$ modified |

> [!NOTE]
> In FEM parlance, **essential** BCs (Dirichlet) are imposed *before* solving the system $\mathbf{K u} = \mathbf{F}$, while **natural** BCs (Neumann) are automatically satisfied by the weak form and only enter the load vector.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Equation / Formula** | **Type of PDE** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady heat conduction (with source) | $\nabla^2 T + \dfrac{Q}{k} = 0$ | Poisson (Elliptic) |
| 2 | Steady heat conduction (no source) | $\nabla^2 T = 0$ | Laplace (Elliptic) |
| 3 | Transient heat conduction | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T + \dfrac{Q}{\rho c_p}$ | Parabolic |
| 4 | Torsion (Prandtl function) | $\nabla^2 \phi = -2G\theta$ | Poisson (Elliptic) |
| 5 | Irrotational 2D flow | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ | Laplace (Elliptic) |
| 6 | Linear elasticity equilibrium | $\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} + \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$ | System of Elliptic |
| 7 | Wave propagation | $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \nabla^2 u$ | Hyperbolic |
| 8 | Diffusion equation | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D \nabla^2 u$ | Parabolic |
| 9 | Electrostatic potential (Poisson) | $\nabla^2 V = -\dfrac{\rho_e}{\varepsilon}$ | Poisson (Elliptic) |
| 10 | Membrane deflection | $T \nabla^2 w = -p(x,y)$ | Poisson (Elliptic) |

### PDE Classification Criterion (Second-Order Linear PDE)

For the general 2D second-order PDE:

$$
A \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + B \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial y} + C \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + D \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + E \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + F u = G
$$

- **Elliptic:** $B^2 - 4AC < 0$ → Steady-state equilibrium (Laplace, Poisson)
- **Parabolic:** $B^2 - 4AC = 0$ → Diffusion-type (heat, mass transfer)
- **Hyperbolic:** $B^2 - 4AC > 0$ → Wave-type (vibrations, electromagnetics)

## Why is Mathematical Modelling Critical?

> [!NOTE]
> Without a sound mathematical model, the **Finite Element Method** has no foundation. The PDE dictates the *form* of the stiffness matrix $\mathbf{K}$, the *type* of element shape functions required, and the *stability* constraints on the discretisation. In engineering, this modelling step bridges the gap between *physics* and *computation*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Heat Conduction Equation from Energy Conservation

We derive the governing equation for transient heat conduction in a solid by applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to an infinitesimal control volume $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$.

**Step 1: Rate of heat accumulation within the volume:**

The internal energy per unit volume is $\rho c_p T$. Thus the rate of energy storage is:

$$
\dot{E}_{storage} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho c_p T) \, dV = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \, dV
$$

**Step 2: Net rate of heat inflow by conduction (Fourier's Law):**

The heat flux vector is $\mathbf{q} = -k \nabla T$. The net inflow through the control volume surface is:

$$
\dot{E}_{in} = -\oint_{\partial V} \mathbf{q} \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS = \oint_{\partial V} k (\nabla T) \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Divergence Theorem** to convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$
\oint_{\partial V} k (\nabla T) \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS = \int_V \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) \, dV
$$

**Step 4: Internal heat generation** $Q$ (W/m³):

$$
\dot{E}_{gen} = \int_V Q \, dV
$$

**Step 5: Apply conservation (storage = inflow + generation):**

$$
\int_V \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \, dV = \int_V \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) \, dV + \int_V Q \, dV
$$

**Step 6: Since this holds for an arbitrary $dV$, the integrands must be equal:**

$$
\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + Q
$$

**Step 7: For constant $k$ (homogeneous isotropic medium):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) &= k \nabla \cdot (\nabla T) = k \nabla^2 T \\
\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} &= k \nabla^2 T + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Divide throughout by $\rho c_p$ to obtain the canonical form:**

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T + \frac{Q}{\rho c_p}
$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ is the **thermal diffusivity**.

## Derivation 2: Derivation of the Torsion Equation (Prandtl's Stress Function)

Consider a straight prismatic shaft of arbitrary cross-section twisted about its longitudinal axis by a torque $T$. Let $\theta$ be the angle of twist per unit length.

**Step 1: Displacement field** for a point $(x, y, z)$:

$$
u = -\theta z y, \quad v = \theta z x, \quad w = 0
$$

**Step 2: Strain tensor** (only non-zero components):

$$
\varepsilon_{xz} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = -\frac{\theta y}{2}, \quad \varepsilon_{yz} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = \frac{\theta x}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Stress tensor** (using shear modulus $G$):

$$
\tau_{xz} = G \varepsilon_{xz} = -\frac{G \theta y}{2}, \quad \tau_{yz} = G \varepsilon_{yz} = \frac{G \theta x}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Define the Prandtl stress function** $\phi(x,y)$ such that:

$$
\tau_{xz} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}, \quad \tau_{yz} = -\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 5: Apply equilibrium** $\dfrac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial y} = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0 \ \checkmark \quad \text{(automatically satisfied)}
$$

**Step 6: Apply compatibility** $\dfrac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial y} - \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yz}}{\partial x} = -2 G \theta$:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = -2 G \theta
$$

This yields the **Poisson equation**:

$$
\nabla^2 \phi = -2 G \theta
$$

**Step 7: Boundary condition** — On the free surface of the shaft, the shear stress must be tangent to the boundary, implying $\phi = 0$ on $\Gamma$.

**Step 8: Torque** is obtained from:

$$
T = 2 \int_\Omega \phi \, dA
$$

## Derivation 3: Classification of Second-Order PDEs

Consider a general second-order linear PDE in two independent variables:

$$
A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} + D u_x + E u_y + F u = G
$$

**Step 1: Transform to characteristic coordinates** $(\xi, \eta)$ to eliminate cross-derivative. The characteristic directions are given by:

$$
A \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 - 2B \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right) + C = 0
$$

**Step 2: Discriminant of the quadratic:**

$$
\Delta = (2B)^2 - 4AC = 4(B^2 - AC)
$$

**Step 3: Classification by sign of $B^2 - AC$:**

- If $B^2 - AC < 0$: **Elliptic** (no real characteristics) → boundary value problems, e.g., $\nabla^2 u = 0$
- If $B^2 - AC = 0$: **Parabolic** (one repeated real characteristic) → initial-boundary value, e.g., $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$
- If $B^2 - AC > 0$: **Hyperbolic** (two real distinct characteristics) → wave propagation, e.g., $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$

## Derivation 4: Weak Form of a 1D Steady Field Problem (Foundation of FEM)

Consider a 1D steady field problem:

$$
-\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{du}{dx}\right) + cu = f, \quad 0 < x < L
$$

with BCs: $u(0) = u_0$ (essential) and $k \dfrac{du}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = q_L$ (natural).

**Step 1: Multiply the PDE by an arbitrary *test function* $w(x)$ and integrate:**

$$
\int_0^L w \left[-\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{du}{dx}\right) + cu - f\right] dx = 0
$$

**Step 2: Apply integration by parts** to the second-order term:

$$
\int_0^L w \left(-\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{du}{dx}\right)\right) dx = -\left[w k \frac{du}{dx}\right]_0^L + \int_0^L \frac{dw}{dx} k \frac{du}{dx} \, dx
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and apply natural BCs** (the term at $x = L$ becomes $w(L) \cdot q_L$, while at $x = 0$, $w(0) = 0$ for admissible test functions):

$$
\int_0^L \left(k \frac{dw}{dx}\frac{du}{dx} + c w u - w f\right) dx = w(L) q_L
$$

This is the **weak (variational) form** of the original PDE — the foundation on which the FEM is built. The function $u$ must be once continuously differentiable (weaker than the second derivative requirement of the original PDE).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Workflow of Mathematical Modelling of a Field Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Engineering Problem] --> B[Idealisation and Assumptions]
    B --> C[Define Domain Omega and Boundary Gamma]
    C --> D[Select Field Variable u x t]
    D --> E[Apply Conservation Law]
    E --> F[Derive Governing PDE]
    F --> G[Specify Boundary Conditions]
    G --> H[Specify Initial Conditions if Transient]
    H --> I[Mathematical Model BVP or IBVP]
    I --> J{Analytical or Numerical Solution}
    J -->|Numerical| K[Apply FEM or FDM or FVM]
    J -->|Analytical| L[Separation of Variables or Transform Methods]
    K --> M[Approximate Solution]
    L --> N[Exact Solution]
    M --> O[Post Processing and Interpretation]
    N --> O
```

## Diagram 2: Classification of Boundary Conditions in Field Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boundary Conditions on Gamma] --> B[Dirichlet Essential]
    A --> C[Neumann Natural]
    A --> D[Robin Mixed or Cauchy]
    B --> B1[u equals u bar on Gamma u]
    B --> B2[Example: Fixed temperature T equals T0]
    C --> C1[Flux q equals q bar on Gamma q]
    C --> C2[Example: Insulated boundary dT dx equals 0]
    D --> D1[Combined ku over n plus h times u minus u infinity equals 0]
    D --> D2[Example: Convective heat loss at surface]
```

## Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture — FEM Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Mathematical Model PDE and BC] --> A2[Weak Formulation via Galerkin]
    A2 --> A3[Domain Discretisation into Elements]
    A3 --> A4[Shape Function Approximation]
    A4 --> A5[Element Stiffness Matrix and Load Vector]
    A5 --> A6[Assembly of Global System K U equals F]
    A6 --> A7[Application of Boundary Conditions]
    A7 --> A8[Solution of Linear or Nonlinear System]
    A8 --> A9[Post Processing Stresses Fluxes]
```

## Diagram 4: PDE Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Second Order Linear PDE] --> B[Compute B squared minus 4 A C]
    B --> C{Value of Discriminant}
    C -->|Less than 0| D[Elliptic]
    C -->|Equals 0| E[Parabolic]
    C -->|Greater than 0| F[Hyperbolic]
    D --> D1[Examples: Laplace Poisson Steady Heat Elasticity]
    E --> E1[Examples: Heat Equation Diffusion Transient]
    F --> F1[Examples: Wave Equation Vibrations Electromagnetics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams collectively map the entire thought process — from the **physical reality** to the **mathematical statement** to the **numerical solution strategy**. The KTU examiner often awards partial marks (up to 3/14) for clear flowcharts in descriptive answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between essential (Dirichlet) and natural (Neumann) boundary conditions with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Essential Boundary Conditions (Dirichlet):** These conditions specify the value of the field variable itself on a portion of the boundary. They are called "essential" because they must be imposed *before* the FEM system is solved — they are not automatically satisfied by the weak form. The trial functions are chosen to satisfy these conditions a priori.

- **Mathematical form:** $u = \bar{u}$ on $\Gamma_u$
- **Example 1:** In heat conduction, the temperature is prescribed on a surface: $T = T_0$ on $\Gamma_T$.
- **Example 2:** In structural mechanics, the displacement is fixed: $\mathbf{u} = \bar{\mathbf{u}}$ on $\Gamma_u$.
- **Example 3:** In electrostatics, the potential is grounded: $V = 0$ on a conductor.

**Natural Boundary Conditions (Neumann):** These conditions specify the *flux* (derivative) of the field variable normal to the boundary. They are called "natural" because they appear automatically in the weak form and contribute to the load vector $\mathbf{F}$ without modification of the stiffness matrix.

- **Mathematical form:** $k \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = \bar{q}$ on $\Gamma_q$ (or $-k \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = \bar{q}$ depending on sign convention)
- **Example 1:** Insulated boundary in heat conduction: $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n} = 0$.
- **Example 2:** Traction-free surface in elasticity: $\boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \mathbf{n} = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Example 3:** Specified heat flux on a furnace wall: $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n} = q_0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "natural conditions are *optional*" — this is **WRONG**. They are equally necessary for a well-posed problem. They are *natural* because of the variational formulation, not because of their importance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Classify the following PDEs as Elliptic, Parabolic, or Hyperbolic: (i) $\nabla^2 u = 0$, (ii) $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$, (iii) $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$. Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

The classification of a second-order linear PDE $A u_{xx} + 2B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} = 0$ depends on the sign of the discriminant $B^2 - AC$.

**(i) Laplace Equation** $\nabla^2 u = \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0$

Here $A = 1$, $B = 0$, $C = 1$. Discriminant: $B^2 - AC = 0 - (1)(1) = -1 < 0$. **→ Elliptic.**

This represents steady-state field problems (irrotational flow, electrostatic field in charge-free region, steady heat conduction, torsion).

**(ii) Heat Equation** $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$

This is first-order in $t$ and second-order in $x$. Reducing to standard form with $t$ treated analogous to $y$: $A = \alpha$, $B = 0$, $C = 0$. Discriminant: $B^2 - AC = 0 - 0 = 0$. **→ Parabolic.**

This represents time-dependent diffusion processes (transient heat conduction, mass diffusion, groundwater flow).

**(iii) Wave Equation** $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$

Rewriting: $c^2 u_{xx} - u_{tt} = 0$. Here $A = c^2$, $B = 0$, $C = -1$. Discriminant: $B^2 - AC = 0 - (c^2)(-1) = c^2 > 0$. **→ Hyperbolic.**

This represents wave propagation phenomena (vibrating strings, sound, electromagnetic waves, shock waves).

> [!NOTE]
> The **physical nature** of the problem dictates the classification: equilibrium (Elliptic), diffusion (Parabolic), propagation (Hyperbolic).

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 1 carries one compulsory 14-mark question with **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the governing differential equation for one-dimensional transient heat conduction in a solid rod, starting from energy conservation applied to an elemental control volume. Clearly state the assumptions and identify each term in the final equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A long cylindrical rod of radius $R$ and thermal conductivity $k$ is subjected to a uniform internal heat generation $Q$ (W/m³) and has its surface maintained at temperature $T_s$. Steady-state conditions prevail. Set up the mathematical model (governing equation + boundary conditions) for the radial temperature distribution $T(r)$ in the rod.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. One-dimensional heat flow (along the $x$-axis).
2. Isotropic, homogeneous material with constant properties $k$, $\rho$, $c_p$.
3. No internal heat generation in the elemental volume considered.
4. No radiation; conduction is the only mode.

**Derivation:**

Consider an elemental slice of the rod between $x$ and $x + dx$ with cross-sectional area $A$. Energy balance on this element:

$$
\text{Energy In} - \text{Energy Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Energy Stored}
$$

**Step 1:** Heat conducted in at $x$ (Fourier's Law):

$$
q_x = -k A \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_x
$$

**Step 2:** Heat conducted out at $x + dx$:

$$
q_{x+dx} = -k A \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x+dx}
$$

**Step 3:** Taylor expansion of $q_{x+dx}$ about $x$:

$$
q_{x+dx} = q_x + \frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} dx = -kA \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(kA\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) dx
$$

**Step 4:** Net heat inflow into the element:

$$
q_x - q_{x+dx} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(kA\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) dx
$$

**Step 5:** Rate of energy storage (mass $\times$ specific heat $\times$ rate of temperature change):

$$
\text{Storage} = (\rho A dx) c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 6:** Equate and simplify** (divide by $A \, dx$):

$$
\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** For constant $k$:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}
$$

where $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ is the thermal diffusivity. Each term has units $\mathrm{K/s}$, confirming dimensional consistency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Identification of Terms:**
> - $\rho c_p \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t}$: rate of energy storage per unit volume
> - $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)$: net heat influx by conduction
> - $\alpha$: thermal diffusivity (a material property governing the *speed* of heat propagation)

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the field variable and domain.**
Field variable: $T(r)$ (radial temperature, axisymmetry assumed). Domain: $0 \leq r \leq R$, $0 \leq z \leq L$.

**Step 2: Governing equation in cylindrical coordinates** (steady-state, 1D radial, with source $Q$):

For axisymmetric problems, the Laplacian in cylindrical coordinates reduces to:

$$
\nabla^2 T = \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right)
$$

Applying steady-state energy balance:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(k r \frac{dT}{dr}\right) + Q = 0
$$

For constant $k$:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\frac{Q}{k}
$$

**Step 3: Boundary conditions:**

- **At $r = 0$ (centre, symmetry):** The temperature must be finite and the gradient zero by symmetry:
$$
\frac{dT}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=0} = 0 \quad \text{(Neumann / natural)}
$$

- **At $r = R$ (surface):** Temperature is prescribed:
$$
T(R) = T_s \quad \text{(Dirichlet / essential)}
$$

**Step 4: Solve by direct integration:**

$$
\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\frac{Q r}{k}
$$

Integrating once:

$$
r \frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{Q r^2}{2k} + C_1
$$

$$
\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{Q r}{2k} + \frac{C_1}{r}
$$

Applying the BC at $r = 0$ (regularity demands $C_1 = 0$):

$$
\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{Q r}{2k}
$$

Integrating again:

$$
T(r) = -\frac{Q r^2}{4k} + C_2
$$

**Step 5: Apply BC at $r = R$:**

$$
T_s = -\frac{Q R^2}{4k} + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = T_s + \frac{Q R^2}{4k}
$$

**Step 6: Final temperature distribution:**

$$
T(r) = T_s + \frac{Q}{4k}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)
$$

**Step 7: Maximum temperature** at $r = 0$:

$$
T_{max} = T(0) = T_s + \frac{Q R^2}{4k}
$$

**Step 8: Mathematical model summary:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{PDE:} \quad \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{Q}{k} = 0, \quad 0 < r < R \\[4pt]
&\text{BC1:} \quad \frac{dT}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=0} = 0 \\[4pt]
&\text{BC2:} \quad T(R) = T_s
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the procedure of mathematical modelling of a field problem. Identify the governing differential equation, boundary conditions, and initial conditions for the transient heat conduction problem in a fin of rectangular cross-section.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thin rectangular plate of dimensions $L \times H$ is subjected to a uniform transverse load $p$ (N/m²) on its top surface and is simply supported on all four edges. Using the classical thin plate theory (Kirchhoff-Love hypothesis), derive the governing differential equation for the transverse deflection $w(x,y)$ and state the boundary conditions on all four edges.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Procedure of Mathematical Modelling** (refer to the flow diagram in Section 4 as visual aid).

The steps are:
1. Identify the physical problem
2. Define domain $\Omega$ and boundary $\Gamma$
3. Choose field variable(s)
4. Apply conservation law → derive PDE
5. Specify boundary conditions
6. Specify initial conditions (if transient)
7. Solve the resulting BVP/IBVP

**Application to a Rectangular Fin:**

A fin of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$ is attached to a base at temperature $T_b$. The fin loses heat by convection to the ambient at $T_\infty$ with coefficient $h$. The base end ($x = 0$) is hot, the tip ($x = L$) may be insulated, convective, or prescribed in temperature.

- **Domain:** $0 \leq x \leq L$, $0 \leq y \leq b$, $0 \leq z \leq t$.
- **Assumptions:** 1D heat flow (along $x$), constant $k$, uniform cross-section $A_c = b t$, perimeter $P = 2(b + t)$, no internal heat generation.

**Governing Differential Equation:**

Energy balance on element $dx$:

$$
\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - m^2 (T - T_\infty) = 0
$$

where $m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{k A_c}$ is the fin parameter.

**Boundary Conditions:**

| **End** | **Condition Type** | **Mathematical Form** |
|---|---|---|
| Base ($x = 0$) | Dirichlet (essential) | $T(0) = T_b$ |
| Tip ($x = L$), insulated | Neumann (natural) | $\dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ |
| Tip ($x = L$), convective | Robin (mixed) | $-k \dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = h(T(L) - T_\infty)$ |
| Tip ($x = L$), prescribed | Dirichlet | $T(L) = T_L$ |

**Initial Conditions:** For transient fin analysis, $T(x, 0) = T_i(x)$ for all $x \in [0, L]$.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1: Kirchhoff-Love Assumptions:**
1. The plate is thin: $t \ll L, H$.
2. Straight lines normal to the mid-surface remain straight and normal after deformation.
3. The thickness does not change (no transverse normal strain).
4. Transverse shear deformation is neglected.

**Step 2: Curvatures and Moments:**

The strain field is:

$$
\varepsilon_x = -z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}, \quad \varepsilon_y = -z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = -2z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}
$$

Bending moments per unit length:

$$
M_x = -D\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}\right)
$$

$$
M_y = -D\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}\right)
$$

$$
M_{xy} = -D(1 - \nu) \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}
$$

where $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate.

**Step 3: Equilibrium (vertical direction):**

$$
\frac{\partial^2 M_x}{\partial x^2} + 2\frac{\partial^2 M_{xy}}{\partial x \partial y} + \frac{\partial^2 M_y}{\partial y^2} = -p(x, y)
$$

**Step 4: Substituting moment expressions and simplifying:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&-D \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}\right) \\
&- 2D(1-\nu) \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x \partial y}\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}\right) \\
&- D \frac{\partial^2}{\partial y^2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}\right) = -p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Biharmonic form:**

$$
\nabla^4 w = \frac{p}{D}
$$

where $\nabla^4 = \nabla^2 \nabla^2 = \dfrac{\partial^4}{\partial x^4} + 2\dfrac{\partial^4}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^4}{\partial y^4}$ is the **biharmonic operator**.

**Step 6: Boundary conditions on the simply supported edges:**

For a **simply supported** edge, e.g., at $x = 0$ and $x = L$:
- Deflection vanishes: $w = 0$ (Dirichlet)
- Bending moment vanishes: $M_x = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} = 0$ (Natural/Moment BC)

Similarly at $y = 0$ and $y = H$:
- $w = 0$ and $M_y = 0$.

**Mathematical model summary:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{PDE:} \quad D \nabla^4 w = p(x, y), \quad 0 < x < L, \ 0 < y < H \\[4pt]
&\text{BC at } x = 0, L: \quad w = 0, \ \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} = 0 \\[4pt]
&\text{BC at } y = 0, H: \quad w = 0, \ \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} = 0
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Kirchhoff plate theory** (4th-order PDE, $\nabla^4 w = p/D$) with **membrane theory** (2nd-order PDE, $T \nabla^2 w = -p$). The order of the PDE has critical implications for FEM: a 4th-order PDE requires **$C^1$-continuous** Hermite elements (with derivatives as nodal unknowns), whereas a 2nd-order PDE needs only **$C^0$-continuous** Lagrange elements. Marking will explicitly test this distinction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Mathematical Modelling of Field Problems**

- ☐ A **field problem** is governed by a PDE over a continuous domain $\Omega$ with boundary $\Gamma$.
- ☐ The **general statement** of a field problem is $\mathcal{L}(u) = f$ in $\Omega$, $\mathcal{B}(u) = g$ on $\Gamma$.
- ☐ A well-posed problem requires **existence**, **uniqueness**, and **continuous dependence** on data (Hadamard condition).
- ☐ The **three steps** in FEM-friendly modelling: (1) PDE, (2) Boundary Conditions, (3) Initial Conditions (if transient).
- ☐ **Dirichlet (Essential) BCs** prescribe $u = \bar{u}$; they modify the stiffness matrix $\mathbf{K}$.
- ☐ **Neumann (Natural) BCs** prescribe the flux $\partial u/\partial n$; they modify the load vector $\mathbf{F}$.
- ☐ **Robin (Mixed/Cauchy) BCs** combine both $u$ and $\partial u/\partial n$; they modify both $\mathbf{K}$ and $\mathbf{F}$.
- ☐ The **Laplace equation** $\nabla^2 u = 0$ governs steady-state, source-free problems.
- ☐ The **Poisson equation** $\nabla^2 u = -f$ governs steady-state problems with sources.
- ☐ The **heat equation** $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \nabla^2 T + Q/(\rho c_p)$ governs transient heat conduction.
- ☐ The **wave equation** $\partial^2 u/\partial t^2 = c^2 \nabla^2 u$ governs wave propagation.
- ☐ PDEs are classified via $B^2 - 4AC$: Elliptic (< 0), Parabolic (= 0), Hyperbolic (> 0).
- ☐ The **Prandtl stress function** for torsion satisfies $\nabla^2 \phi = -2G\theta$ with $\phi = 0$ on $\Gamma$.
- ☐ The **biharmonic equation** $\nabla^4 w = p/D$ governs thin plate bending (Kirchhoff theory).
- ☐ **Thermal diffusivity** $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ governs the rate of heat propagation.
- ☐ **Flexural rigidity** $D = Et^3/[12(1 - \nu^2)]$ is the plate bending analogue of $EA$ for bars.
- ☐ The **weak form** (obtained via integration by parts / Galerkin) is the entry point for FEM.
- ☐ **Hermite elements** (with continuous first derivatives) are required for 4th-order PDEs.
- ☐ **Lagrange elements** (with continuous function values only) suffice for 2nd-order PDEs.
- ☐ Mathematical modelling is **domain-specific**: each physical phenomenon has its own conservation law.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Mathematical Modelling of Field Problems in Engineering**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Governing Equations - Discrete and Continuous Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET723 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Governing Equations - Discrete and Continuous Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Finite Element Method (FEM), a **governing equation** is the mathematical statement (typically a differential equation or a set of differential equations) that a physical field variable must satisfy throughout the domain of interest, along with the prescribed conditions on the boundary of that domain. The transition from a physical problem to a mathematically solvable model can be formulated either as a **continuous model** (where the field variable is defined at every infinitesimal point of the domain) or as a **discrete model** (where the field variable is defined only at a finite set of points, or degrees of freedom).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 Highlight)**
> A *continuous model* preserves the continuity of the field variable across the entire domain and is represented by a **strong form** (a point-wise differential equation) or its equivalent **weak form** (an integral statement). A *discrete model* approximates the field by assuming prescribed shapes (shape functions) over a finite number of sub-domains called **elements**, connected at **nodes**.

The two principal mathematical representations of a governing equation are:

| Representation | Mathematical Nature | Continuity Requirement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Strong Form** | Point-wise differential equation (e.g., $\frac{d^2u}{dx^2} + f = 0$) | Field must be differentiable up to the order of the highest derivative |
| **Weak Form** | Integral statement (e.g., weighted residual / variational) | Field needs only first-order differentiability in the integral sense |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are describing the temperature distribution along a **1-metre metal rod** whose one end is held in ice ($0^\circ$C) and the other end is placed in boiling water ($100^\circ$C).

- **Continuous Model Analogy:** You are asked to specify the temperature at *every single point* along the rod — infinitely many values, one for each location $x \in [0, 1]$. This is a continuous description, governed by the steady-state heat equation $\frac{d^2T}{dx^2} = 0$, whose exact solution is the straight line $T(x) = 100x$. Nothing is approximated; the rod is described in its entirety.

- **Discrete Model Analogy:** You are instead told: *"Measure the temperature only at 5 equally-spaced points (nodes) along the rod, and assume the temperature varies linearly between them."* You now have only **5 unknowns** (the nodal temperatures), and the rod is approximated by **4 straight segments (elements)**. This is the essence of FEM — dividing the continuum into a finite number of pieces and solving for a finite number of unknowns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The governing equation itself (e.g., Poisson's equation, equilibrium equation, Euler-Bernoulli beam equation) is a *property of the physics*. The distinction between *discrete* and *continuous* lies purely in **how we choose to represent and solve** that governing equation.

## 1.3 Standard Physical / Mathematical Constants Used

The following constants frequently appear in the governing equations of standard FEM problems:

- **Young's Modulus** $E$ — measured in **N/m²** or **Pa** ($\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$).
- **Poisson's Ratio** $\nu$ — dimensionless, typically $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Conductivity** $k$ — measured in **W/(m·K)**.
- **Density** $\rho$ — measured in **kg/m³**.
- **Acceleration due to gravity** $g \approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ — relevant in thermodynamic FEM problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuous Model — Strong Form

A continuous model in FEM begins with the **strong form** of the governing equation, also called the **classical form**. For a 1-D domain $\Omega = [0, L]$ with boundary $\Gamma$, the canonical 1-D second-order ODE is:

$$
-\frac{d}{dx}\left(a(x)\frac{du}{dx}\right) + c(x)\,u = f(x), \quad x \in \Omega
$$

subject to **boundary conditions**:

- **Essential (Dirichlet) boundary condition** on $\Gamma_u$: $u = \bar{u}$
- **Natural (Neumann) boundary condition** on $\Gamma_q$: $a \frac{du}{dx} = \bar{q}$ (flux prescribed)

The *strong form* demands that the equation be satisfied **point-wise** at every $x \in \Omega$, and the solution $u$ must be at least $C^1$-continuous (continuously differentiable up to first derivative) for the derivative to be defined.

### Why we move to the weak form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In FEM, the **strong form is rarely solved directly** because:
> 1. Engineering geometries are complex and analytical solutions are usually impossible.
> 2. The continuity requirement ($C^1$ or higher) is too strict for piecewise-polynomial approximations.
> 3. The weak form *weakens* the continuity requirement to $C^0$, which is exactly what standard shape functions (linear, quadratic) provide.

## 2.2 The Weak Form — Foundation of FEM

Starting from the strong form, we multiply by an arbitrary **weight (test) function** $w(x)$ that vanishes on the essential boundary $\Gamma_u$, and integrate over the domain $\Omega$:

$$
\int_{0}^{L} w\left[-\frac{d}{dx}\left(a\frac{du}{dx}\right) + c\,u - f\right] dx = 0
$$

Applying **integration by parts** (Green's theorem in 1-D) to the second-derivative term, we obtain the **weak form** (also called the **Galerkin form** when the test function equals the trial function):

$$
\int_{0}^{L} \left[ a\frac{du}{dx}\frac{dw}{dx} + c\,u\,w \right] dx = \int_{0}^{L} f\,w\,dx + \left[ w\,a\frac{du}{dx} \right]_{0}^{L}
$$

This is the cornerstone of FEM. The order of differentiation on $u$ has been **reduced by one**, so $u$ need only be $C^0$-continuous (continuous, but not necessarily smooth at element boundaries).

## 2.3 The Discrete Model — Galerkin Approximation

In the discrete model, the continuous field $u(x)$ is approximated by a finite sum over a set of basis (shape) functions $N_i(x)$ and nodal unknowns $d_i$:

$$
u(x) \approx u^h(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x)\,d_i
$$

where $n$ is the total number of nodes. The **trial functions** $N_i(x)$ are typically low-order polynomials (linear, quadratic, or cubic) defined locally over each element. Substituting this approximation into the weak form yields a system of **linear algebraic equations**:

$$
[K]\{d\} = \{F\}
$$

where $[K]$ is the **global stiffness matrix**, $\{d\}$ is the vector of **nodal unknowns**, and $\{F\}$ is the **global load vector**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarises the governing equations, weak forms, and corresponding discrete (matrix) forms for the **three most important 1-D problems** in KTU Module 1.

| Problem | Strong Form | Weak Form | Discrete Matrix Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Axial Bar / 1-D Heat Conduction** | $-\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\dfrac{du}{dx}\right) = f$ | $\int_0^L EA\,\dfrac{du}{dx}\dfrac{dw}{dx}\,dx = \int_0^L f\,w\,dx + \left[w\,\bar{q}\right]_0^L$ | $[K]\{d\} = \{F\}$, with $K_{ij} = \sum_e \int_{e} EA\,\dfrac{dN_i}{dx}\dfrac{dN_j}{dx}\,dx$ |
| **Torsion of Circular Shaft** | $-\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(GJ\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\right) = T$ | $\int_0^L GJ\,\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\dfrac{dw}{dx}\,dx = \int_0^L T\,w\,dx + \left[w\,\bar{T}\right]_0^L$ | $K_{ij} = \sum_e \int_{e} GJ\,\dfrac{dN_i}{dx}\dfrac{dN_j}{dx}\,dx$ |
| **Euler-Bernoulli Beam** | $\dfrac{d^2}{dx^2}\!\left(EI\dfrac{d^2w}{dx^2}\right) = q$ | $\int_0^L EI\,\dfrac{d^2w}{dx^2}\dfrac{d^2v}{dx^2}\,dx = \int_0^L q\,v\,dx$ | $K_{ij} = \sum_e \int_{e} EI\,\dfrac{d^2N_i}{dx^2}\dfrac{d^2N_j}{dx^2}\,dx$ (Hermite shape fns.) |

### Types of Boundary Conditions Encountered

| Type | Name | Example (1-D Heat) | Mathematical Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Essential** | Dirichlet | Temperature prescribed | $u = \bar{u}$ on $\Gamma_u$ |
| **Natural** | Neumann | Heat flux prescribed | $a\dfrac{du}{dx} = \bar{q}$ on $\Gamma_q$ |
| **Mixed** | Robin / Cauchy | Convection at surface | $a\dfrac{du}{dx} + h\,u = h\,u_\infty$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The discrete-vs-continuous model distinction is **not just academic** — it directly controls the design process in industry:

- **Aerospace**: Stress analysis of aircraft wings uses millions of elements in the discrete model to capture complex loading and geometry.
- **Civil Engineering**: Settlement analysis of foundations uses continuous models (e.g., Boussinesq's solution) for quick estimates, but switches to discrete FEM models for irregular soil profiles.
- **Biomedical**: Heat transfer in hyperthermia cancer treatment uses FEM discrete models to predict tissue temperature distributions.
- **Automotive Crashworthiness**: Discrete FEM models with explicit time integration are used to simulate crash events, where the continuous model would be analytically intractable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Strong Form to Weak Form (1-D Poisson-Type Equation)

### Given

Consider a 1-D domain $\Omega = [0, L]$. The **strong form** of the governing equation is:

$$
-\frac{d}{dx}\left(a(x)\frac{du}{dx}\right) + c(x)\,u(x) = f(x), \quad x \in (0, L)
$$

with boundary conditions:
- $u(0) = \bar{u}_0$ (essential)
- $u(L) = \bar{u}_L$ (essential)

For the homogeneous case, assume the test function $w(x)$ satisfies $w(0) = w(L) = 0$.

### Step 1 — Multiply by an Arbitrary Test Function $w(x)$

We multiply the strong form by $w(x)$ (which is zero at both boundaries) and integrate over the entire domain $\Omega$:

$$
\int_{0}^{L} w(x)\left[-\frac{d}{dx}\left(a\frac{du}{dx}\right) + c\,u - f\right] dx = 0
$$

This statement must hold for *all* admissible $w(x)$. The reason this works: if the original equation were violated at any single point, a sufficiently narrow test function (a "bump") would make the integral non-zero, contradicting the statement.

### Step 2 — Split the Integral

Expanding the integrand into three separate integrals:

$$
-\int_{0}^{L} w\,\frac{d}{dx}\left(a\frac{du}{dx}\right) dx + \int_{0}^{L} c\,u\,w\,dx - \int_{0}^{L} f\,w\,dx = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Integration by Parts

We focus on the first integral. Recall the product rule for differentiation:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\left(w \cdot a\frac{du}{dx}\right) = \frac{dw}{dx}\cdot a\frac{du}{dx} + w\cdot\frac{d}{dx}\left(a\frac{du}{dx}\right)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
w\cdot\frac{d}{dx}\left(a\frac{du}{dx}\right) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(w\,a\frac{du}{dx}\right) - \frac{dw}{dx}\cdot a\frac{du}{dx}
$$

Substitute this into the first integral:

$$
-\int_{0}^{L}\left[\frac{d}{dx}\left(w\,a\frac{du}{dx}\right) - \frac{dw}{dx}\,a\frac{du}{dx}\right] dx
$$

Split into two pieces:

$$
-\int_{0}^{L}\frac{d}{dx}\left(w\,a\frac{du}{dx}\right) dx + \int_{0}^{L} a\frac{du}{dx}\frac{dw}{dx}\,dx
$$

The first piece is a perfect derivative. Integrating gives the boundary term (Fundamental Theorem of Calculus):

$$
-\left[w\,a\frac{du}{dx}\right]_{0}^{L} + \int_{0}^{L} a\frac{du}{dx}\frac{dw}{dx}\,dx
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Boundary Conditions on $w$

Since $w(0) = w(L) = 0$ (because $u$ is prescribed at both ends, the variation $w$ must vanish there), the boundary term vanishes:

$$
\left[w\,a\frac{du}{dx}\right]_{0}^{L} = w(L)\,a\frac{du}{dx}\bigg|_{L} - w(0)\,a\frac{du}{dx}\bigg|_{0} = 0 - 0 = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the Weak Form

Putting it all together:

$$
\int_{0}^{L} a\frac{du}{dx}\frac{dw}{dx}\,dx + \int_{0}^{L} c\,u\,w\,dx = \int_{0}^{L} f\,w\,dx
$$

$$
\boxed{\int_{0}^{L} \left[ a\frac{du}{dx}\frac{dw}{dx} + c\,u\,w \right] dx = \int_{0}^{L} f\,w\,dx + \left[ w\,\bar{q} \right]_{0}^{L}}
$$

where $\bar{q} = a\dfrac{du}{dx}$ is the prescribed natural boundary flux (non-zero only if a Neumann-type BC is specified).

> [!NOTE]
> **The order of differentiation has been reduced from 2 to 1 on $u$**, and the test function $w$ is only required to be differentiable once. This is the *weakness* of the weak form — and that is precisely why it is more *flexible* for FEM.

## 3.2 Derivation: Discrete Stiffness Matrix for a 2-Node Linear Bar Element

### Given

A bar element of length $L_e$, cross-sectional area $A_e$, Young's modulus $E_e$, with two nodes (1 and 2) at the ends. The axial displacement is approximated linearly:

$$
u(x) = N_1(x)\,d_1 + N_2(x)\,d_2
$$

with linear shape functions on the element $[0, L_e]$:

$$
N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L_e}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L_e}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Derivatives of Shape Functions

$$
\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L_e}, \quad \frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{1}{L_e}
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Weak Form for a Single Element

$$
\int_{0}^{L_e} EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\frac{dw}{dx}\,dx = \int_{0}^{L_e} f\,w\,dx + \text{boundary terms}
$$

With the Galerkin choice $w = N_i$, $u^h = N_j d_j$:

$$
\int_{0}^{L_e} EA\,\left(\sum_{j=1}^{2}\frac{dN_j}{dx}\,d_j\right)\frac{dN_i}{dx}\,dx = \int_{0}^{L_e} f\,N_i\,dx + Q_i
$$

### Step 3 — Isolate the Element Stiffness Matrix

Pulling $d_j$ outside the integral (since it is a constant for the element):

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{2}\left[\int_{0}^{L_e} EA\,\frac{dN_i}{dx}\frac{dN_j}{dx}\,dx\right] d_j = \int_{0}^{L_e} f\,N_i\,dx + Q_i
$$

Define the element stiffness matrix component:

$$
K_{ij}^{(e)} = \int_{0}^{L_e} EA\,\frac{dN_i}{dx}\frac{dN_j}{dx}\,dx
$$

Substitute the derivative values:

$$
K_{11}^{(e)} = \int_{0}^{L_e} EA\left(-\frac{1}{L_e}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{L_e}\right) dx = \frac{EA}{L_e^2}\int_{0}^{L_e} dx = \frac{EA}{L_e}
$$

$$
K_{12}^{(e)} = \int_{0}^{L_e} EA\left(-\frac{1}{L_e}\right)\left(\frac{1}{L_e}\right) dx = -\frac{EA}{L_e}
$$

$$
K_{21}^{(e)} = K_{12}^{(e)} = -\frac{EA}{L_e}
$$

$$
K_{22}^{(e)} = \frac{EA}{L_e}
$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the Element Matrix

$$
[K^{(e)}] = \frac{EA}{L_e}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the famous **2-node bar element stiffness matrix**, which every KTU student is required to memorise.

### Step 5 — Element Load Vector (for Uniform Body Force $f$)

$$
F_i^{(e)} = \int_{0}^{L_e} f\,N_i\,dx
$$

For $N_1 = 1 - x/L_e$:

$$
F_1^{(e)} = \int_{0}^{L_e} f\left(1 - \frac{x}{L_e}\right) dx = f\left[x - \frac{x^2}{2L_e}\right]_{0}^{L_e} = \frac{fL_e}{2}
$$

Similarly:

$$
F_2^{(e)} = \frac{fL_e}{2}
$$

Thus, the element load vector for uniform body force is:

$$
\{F^{(e)}\} = \frac{fL_e}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

## 3.3 Summary: Continuous vs Discrete — Quantitative Comparison

| Feature | Continuous Model (Strong / Weak Form) | Discrete Model (FEM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of Unknowns** | Infinite (function $u(x)$) | Finite ($n$ nodal values) |
| **Solution Type** | Closed-form (analytical) or semi-analytical | Numerical, approximate |
| **Continuity Required** | $C^1$ or $C^2$ (strong) / $C^0$ (weak) | $C^0$ (piecewise polynomial) |
| **Geometry Handling** | Limited to simple shapes | Arbitrary, complex geometries |
| **Computational Cost** | Negligible (analytical) | High (assembly + solve $[K]\{d\}=\{F\}$) |
| **Error** | Exact | Depends on mesh density and element order |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: From Physical Problem to Discrete FEM Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Problem] --> B[Identify Domain and Boundary Conditions]
    B --> C[Derive Governing Differential Equation Strong Form]
    C --> D[Multiply by Test Function w and Integrate over Domain]
    D --> E[Integrate by Parts to Reduce Order of Derivative]
    E --> F[Continuous Weak Form Integral Statement]
    F --> G[Discretize Domain into Elements and Nodes]
    G --> H[Approximate Field by Shape Functions N_i x d_i]
    H --> I[Apply Galerkin Method w = N_i]
    I --> J[Derive Element Stiffness Matrix K_e and Load Vector F_e]
    J --> K[Assemble Global Stiffness Matrix K and Global Load Vector F]
    K --> L[Apply Essential Boundary Conditions u = u_bar]
    L --> M[Solve Linear System K d = F]
    M --> N[Post-Process Nodal Displacements and Derived Quantities]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Comparison Chart: Continuous vs Discrete Model Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph continuousPath[Continuous Model Path]
        C1[Domain Definition] --> C2[Strong Form Differential Equation]
        C2 --> C3[Analytical or Special Function Solution]
        C3 --> C4[Exact Field u exact of x]
    end
    subgraph discretePath[Discrete Model Path]
        D1[Domain Discretization into Elements] --> D2[Element Shape Functions N_i]
        D2 --> D3[Weak Form Integral over Each Element]
        D3 --> D4[Element Stiffness Matrix K_e]
        D4 --> D5[Global Assembly and Solution]
        D5 --> D6[Approximate Nodal Solution d_i]
    end
    continuousPath -. contrast .-> discretePath
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: When to Use Continuous vs Discrete Model
