Shape functions N1, N2"] C --> D["STEP 4: Derive Element Matrices
ke and fe"] D --> E["STEP 5: Global Assembly
K and F"] E --> F["STEP 6: Apply BCs
Dirichlet, Neumann, Convection"] F --> G["STEP 7: Solve K T = F
Nodal Temperatures T"] G --> H["STEP 8: Post-Processing
Heat flux, Gradients"] style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2 style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00 style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2 style D fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C style E fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B style F fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825 style G fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD style H fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F ``` ### 4.3 Functional Minimization Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Functional I(T)
Total Potential Energy"] --> B["Stationary Condition
dI/dTi = 0"] B --> C["Euler Lagrange Equation
Governing PDE"] C --> D["FEM Discretization
Nodal Temperatures"] D --> E["Solve K T = F
Unique Solution"] style A fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#FFF3E0 style C fill:#F3E5F5 style D fill:#E8F5E9 style E fill:#FFEBEE ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define thermal conductivity and state Fourier's law of heat conduction. What is its FEM analogue in structural mechanics?** **Model Answer:** Thermal conductivity $k$ is the material property that quantifies a substance's ability to conduct heat, measured in $\text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$. **Fourier's law** states that the heat flux $q$ through a material is proportional to the negative temperature gradient: $q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$. In structural mechanics, this is analogous to **Hooke's law** $\sigma = E\frac{du}{dx}$, where $k$ corresponds to $E$, $T$ corresponds to displacement $u$, and $q$ corresponds to axial force $N$. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the significance of the weak form in the FEM formulation of a heat conduction problem?** **Model Answer:** The weak form is obtained by multiplying the governing PDE by a weight function and integrating by parts. It is significant because: 1. It **reduces the order of differentiation** by one, requiring only $C^0$ continuity of temperature across elements. 2. It **naturally introduces the boundary flux** term (Neumann BCs) into the formulation. 3. It provides the **basis for the Galerkin approach**, yielding symmetric positive-definite matrices suitable for direct solution. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice #### **Question A (Option 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the element conductivity matrix for a 1D bar element of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and thermal conductivity $k$ subjected to a uniform internal heat generation $\dot{Q}$ per unit volume. Use the Galerkin weighted-residual method. **(b) [7 Marks]** A steel bar of length $0.4 \text{ m}$, $A = 200 \text{ mm}^2$, $k = 45 \text{ W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$ is fixed at $T(0) = 100^\circ\text{C}$ and loses heat by convection at $x = L$ with $h = 200 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$, $T_\infty = 25^\circ\text{C}$. Discretize into 2 equal elements and determine the nodal temperatures. **Model Solution:** **(a) Element Matrix Derivation:** [Refer to Section 3.1 above for the complete 8-step derivation. Final result:] $$[k]^e = \frac{kA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \{f\}^e = \frac{\dot{Q}AL}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Element length** $L_e = 0.2 \text{ m}$. **Element conductivity matrix:** $$[k]^e = \frac{(45)(200 \times 10^{-6})}{0.2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = 0.045 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Convection matrix at node 3** (Robin BC): $h_{conv} = h \cdot A = 200 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 0.04 \text{ W/K}$. **Global matrix with convection contribution:** $$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.045 & -0.045 & 0 \\ -0.045 & 0.090 & -0.045 \\ 0 & -0.045 & 0.045 + 0.04 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.045 & -0.045 & 0 \\ -0.045 & 0.090 & -0.045 \\ 0 & -0.045 & 0.085 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Load vector (convection term at node 3):** $\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ 0 \\ h_{conv} T_\infty \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ 0 \\ 0.04 \times 25 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ 0 \\ 1.0 \end{Bmatrix}$ Applying $T_1 = 100^\circ\text{C}$, the reduced system (rows 2 and 3) becomes: $$0.090\,T_2 - 0.045\,T_3 = 0.045(100) = 4.5$$ $$-0.045\,T_2 + 0.085\,T_3 = 1.0$$ Solving simultaneously (e.g., by matrix inversion or substitution): $$T_2 \approx 80.77^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_3 \approx 54.51^\circ\text{C}$$ --- #### **Question B (Option 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the governing differential equation $\frac{d}{dx}\left(kA\frac{dT}{dx}\right) + \dot{Q}A = 0$, derive the variational (weak) form and the corresponding functional for the 1D heat conduction problem. State clearly the natural and essential boundary conditions that appear in the weak form. **(b) [7 Marks]** An aluminum bar ($k = 200 \text{ W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$, $A = 100 \text{ mm}^2$, $L = 0.3 \text{ m}$) has its left end fixed at $T = 80^\circ\text{C}$ and its right end exposed to a heat flux of $Q_L = 30 \text{ W}$. Internal heat generation is $\dot{Q} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ W/m}^3$. Discretize into 2 equal elements and compute the nodal temperatures. **Model Solution:** **(a) Variational Form Derivation:** Multiplying the governing equation by an arbitrary weight function $W$ and integrating: $$\int_0^L W \left[\frac{d}{dx}\left(kA\frac{dT}{dx}\right) + \dot{Q}A\right] dx = 0$$ Integrate by parts (first term): $$\int_0^L kA\frac{dW}{dx}\frac{dT}{dx} dx - \int_0^L W\dot{Q}A \, dx = \left[W kA\frac{dT}{dx}\right]_0^L$$ The right-hand side is the **boundary term**: - At $x = 0$: gives heat flux $Q_0$ (natural BC). - At $x = L$: gives heat flux $Q_L$ (natural BC). The **functional** whose Euler-Lagrange equation gives the PDE is: $$I[T] = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^L kA\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)^2 dx - \int_0^L \dot{Q}A T \, dx - Q_L T_L + Q_0 T_0$$ The **essential BC** is $T = \bar{T}$ (prescribed temperature). The **natural BC** is $q = \bar{q}$ (prescribed flux), which appears automatically in the weak form. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Element length** $L_e = 0.15 \text{ m}$, **element stiffness:** $$[k]^e = \frac{(200)(100 \times 10^{-6})}{0.15}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = 0.1333 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Element load vector (internal heat generation):** $$\{f\}^e = \frac{\dot{Q}AL_e}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{(5 \times 10^5)(10^{-4})(0.15)}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = 3.75 \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Global Assembly:** $$[K] = 0.1333\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 3.75 \\ 7.5 \\ 3.75 + 30 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 3.75 \\ 7.5 \\ 33.75 \end{Bmatrix}$$ Applying $T_1 = 80^\circ\text{C}$, the reduced system (rows 2 and 3): $$0.2667\,T_2 - 0.1333\,T_3 = 7.5 + 0.1333(80) = 18.167$$ $$-0.1333\,T_2 + 0.1333\,T_3 = 33.75$$ Solving: $$T_2 \approx 218.13^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_3 \approx 471.38^\circ\text{C}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** > 1. **Do not confuse $k$ (thermal conductivity) with $[K]$ (global stiffness matrix).** Many students interchange these symbols in the exam and lose 2 marks immediately. > 2. **Sign convention for Neumann BC:** Heat flux entering the domain is positive. Always include the sign in the load vector carefully. > 3. **Shape function derivative matrix $[B]$** is sometimes mistakenly taken as $[N]$. Remember $[B] = d[N]/dx$. > 4. **Convection BCs** must be added to both the global matrix (as $h \cdot A$ in the diagonal term) AND the load vector (as $h \cdot A \cdot T_\infty$). > 5. **Units check:** $kA/L$ has units of $\text{W/K}$, NOT $\text{W/m}\cdot\text{K}$. Always verify dimensional consistency. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Primary variable** in 1D heat flow: temperature $T(x)$. **Material property:** thermal conductivity $k$. **Governing PDE:** $\frac{d}{dx}(kA\frac{dT}{dx}) + \dot{Q}A = 0$. - **Shape functions** for a 1D linear element: $N_1 = 1 - x/L_e$, $N_2 = x/L_e$. - **Gradient matrix:** $[B] = \begin{bmatrix} -1/L_e & 1/L_e \end{bmatrix}$. - **Element conductivity matrix:** $[k]^e = \frac{kA}{L_e}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — symmetric, positive semi-definite. - **Element load vector (heat source):** $\{f\}^e = \frac{\dot{Q}AL_e}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$. - **Convection contribution:** adds $hA$ to the diagonal entry and $hAT_\infty$ to the load vector at the convective node. - **Neumann BC** (prescribed flux) appears in the load vector; **Dirichlet BC** (prescribed temperature) is applied by row/column elimination. - **Galerkin method** uses shape functions as weight functions — produces symmetric matrices. - **Weak form** reduces the order of differentiation and naturally handles natural (Neumann) boundary conditions. - **Thermal-structural analogy:** $T \leftrightarrow u$, $k \leftrightarrow E$, $q \leftrightarrow N$, $\dot{Q} \leftrightarrow f$ (body force). - **Linear 1D elements** give the **exact solution** for problems with no internal heat source (since the exact solution is itself linear). - **FEM pipeline:** Discretize → Element matrices → Assembly → Apply BCs → Solve → Post-process heat flux $q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$. - **Symmetry and bandwidth:** The global matrix is symmetric and banded; for 1D problems it is tridiagonal. - **Engineering applications:** Fins, heat exchangers, turbine blades, electronic cooling, building thermal insulation, nuclear reactor fuel rods.

topics.title: Natural Coordinate System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET723 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Natural Coordinate System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
The **Natural Coordinate System** (also called the *Intrinsic* or *Isoparametric* local coordinate system) is a dimensionless, element-local coordinate framework used in the Finite Element Method (FEM) to simplify the mathematical formulation of element matrices and to enable the use of standard numerical integration schemes (Gaussian Quadrature).

Unlike the global Cartesian coordinate system **($x, y, z$)**, natural coordinates are dimensionless and bounded — typically by the limits **$-1$** to **$+1$** for one-dimensional line elements and quadrilateral elements, while triangular elements use *area coordinates* bounded by **$0$** to **$1$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The Natural Coordinate System forms the foundation of isoparametric element formulation. Without mastering this, the student cannot proceed to shape function derivation, Jacobian transformations, or numerical integration — all of which are high-weightage areas in KTU Board Examinations.

### Common Natural Coordinate Conventions in FEM

| Element Type | Natural Coordinate(s) | Range | Name |
|--------------|----------------------|-------|------|
| 2-Node Line | $\xi$ | $-1 \le \xi \le +1$ | Natural / Isoparametric |
| 3-Node Triangle | $L_1, L_2, L_3$ | $0 \le L_i \le 1$, $\sum L_i = 1$ | Area Coordinates |
| 4-Node Quadrilateral | $\xi, \eta$ | $-1 \le \xi, \eta \le +1$ | Bilinear Isoparametric |
| 8-Node Quadrilateral | $\xi, \eta$ | $-1 \le \xi, \eta \le +1$ | Serendipity |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Universal Translator":**
> Imagine you have a complex-shaped map of Kerala drawn on an irregular sheet of paper. To measure distances and areas easily, you transfer (or "unwrap") this map onto a perfectly square grid. The grid's coordinates are the **natural coordinates** — clean, uniform, and bounded. The original paper map uses **global coordinates** ($x, y$). The mathematical rule that tells you *exactly* how to convert the square grid back to the irregular paper map is the **Jacobian transformation**. Similarly, in FEM, complex physical elements (curved, skewed, or non-rectangular) are mapped onto a simple parent element (a square, line, or triangle) using natural coordinates.

### Why Natural Coordinates? — The Three Pillars
1. **Bounded Domain:** Natural coordinates always have a fixed range ($-1$ to $+1$ or $0$ to $1$), making numerical integration trivial.
2. **Unified Formulation:** A single generic element (the *parent element*) can be used as a template for any geometry.
3. **Polynomial Shape Functions:** Shape functions become simple polynomials (often linear, quadratic, or cubic) in natural coordinates — much easier to differentiate and integrate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 One-Dimensional Natural Coordinate System

For a 2-node line element with global nodes at positions $x_1$ and $x_2$, the natural coordinate $\xi$ is mapped such that:
- Node 1 corresponds to $\xi = -1$
- Node 2 corresponds to $\xi = +1$

The position of any interior point is interpolated as:
$$x(\xi) = N_1(\xi) x_1 + N_2(\xi) x_2$$

where the **linear shape functions** in natural coordinates are:

$$N_1(\xi) = \frac{1 - \xi}{2}, \quad N_2(\xi) = \frac{1 + \xi}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works (the 'Why'):** Any arbitrary physical line element of length $L = x_2 - x_1$ is mapped onto a "master element" of length 2 (from $-1$ to $+1$). This standardization allows the use of pre-tabulated Gauss points.

### 2.2 Two-Dimensional Natural Coordinate Systems

#### (a) Quadrilateral Elements ($\xi, \eta$)
For a 4-node bilinear quadrilateral, the shape functions are products of 1D shape functions:

$$N_i(\xi, \eta) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi \xi_i)(1 + \eta \eta_i)$$

where $(\xi_i, \eta_i)$ are the natural coordinates of node $i$.

#### (b) Triangular Elements (Area Coordinates $L_1, L_2, L_3$)
For a 3-node constant strain triangle (CST), the natural coordinates are **area ratios**:

$$L_i = \frac{A_i}{A}$$

where $A_i$ is the area of the sub-triangle formed opposite to node $i$, and $A$ is the total element area.

### 2.3 Jacobian Matrix — The Geometric Bridge

The Jacobian matrix $[\mathbf{J}]$ transforms derivatives between natural and global coordinate systems:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial x} \\ \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix} = [\mathbf{J}]^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} \\ \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix}$$

The Jacobian itself is computed from the shape function derivatives with respect to natural coordinates:

$$[\mathbf{J}] = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\ \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} x_i & \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} y_i \\ \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} x_i & \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} y_i \end{bmatrix}$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Range / Constraint | Application |
|-------------------|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1D Linear Shape Function 1 | $N_1 = \frac{1-\xi}{2}$ | $-1 \le \xi \le 1$ | Line element |
| 1D Linear Shape Function 2 | $N_2 = \frac{1+\xi}{2}$ | $-1 \le \xi \le 1$ | Line element |
| 4-Node Quad Shape Function | $N_i = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi\xi_i)(1+\eta\eta_i)$ | $-1 \le \xi, \eta \le 1$ | Quadrilateral |
| Area Coordinate | $L_i = \frac{A_i}{A}$ | $\sum L_i = 1$ | Triangular CST |
| CST Shape Function | $N_i = L_i$ | $0 \le L_i \le 1$ | Triangular |
| Jacobian Matrix (2D) | $[\mathbf{J}] = \begin{bmatrix} \partial x/\partial \xi & \partial y/\partial \xi \\ \partial x/\partial \eta & \partial y/\partial \eta \end{bmatrix}$ | $\det[\mathbf{J}] > 0$ | Coordinate transform |
| Differential Area (Quad) | $dA = \det[\mathbf{J}] \, d\xi \, d\eta$ | Required for $K$ matrix | Integration |
| Differential Area (Tri) | $dA = 2A \, dL_1 \, dL_2$ | $L_3 = 1 - L_1 - L_2$ | Integration |
| Gauss Point (1D, 2-point) | $\xi = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Weights $w = 1$ | Line element |
| Gauss Point (2D, 2x2) | $\xi, \eta = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Weights $w = 1$ | Quadrilateral |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aerospace:** Modeling curved wing surfaces with isoparametric 8-node quadrilaterals.
- **Civil Engineering:** Analyzing dam geometries with triangular CST or LST elements.
- **Biomedical:** Mapping irregular organ geometries (heart, bone) onto standardized parent elements for stress analysis.
- **Automotive Crash Simulation:** Curved panels in car bodies use serendipity elements in natural coordinates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shape Functions for a 4-Node Bilinear Quadrilateral

**Problem Setup:** A 4-node quadrilateral element with global nodal coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$, $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$. We wish to derive the bilinear shape functions in natural coordinates $(\xi, \eta)$.

**Step 1 — Assume a Bilinear Form**
The displacement (or position) field is assumed to be a product of two linear functions in $\xi$ and $\eta$:

$$u(\xi, \eta) = a_1 + a_2 \xi + a_3 \eta + a_4 \xi \eta$$

**Step 2 — Apply Nodal Boundary Conditions**

| Node | $\xi_i$ | $\eta_i$ | $u_i$ |
|------|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 | $-1$ | $-1$ | $u_1$ |
| 2 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $u_2$ |
| 3 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $u_3$ |
| 4 | $-1$ | $+1$ | $u_4$ |

Substituting each node:

$$u_1 = a_1 - a_2 - a_3 + a_4$$
$$u_2 = a_1 + a_2 - a_3 - a_4$$
$$u_3 = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4$$
$$u_4 = a_1 - a_2 + a_3 - a_4$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Linear System**

Adding all four equations:
$$4 a_1 = u_1 + u_2 + u_3 + u_4$$

$$a_1 = \frac{1}{4}(u_1 + u_2 + u_3 + u_4)$$

Solving similarly for $a_2, a_3, a_4$:

$$a_2 = \frac{1}{4}(-u_1 + u_2 + u_3 - u_4)$$
$$a_3 = \frac{1}{4}(-u_1 - u_2 + u_3 + u_4)$$
$$a_4 = \frac{1}{4}(u_1 - u_2 + u_3 - u_4)$$

**Step 4 — Regroup by Nodal DOF**

Substituting back into $u(\xi, \eta)$ and grouping terms by $u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4$:

$$\boxed{u(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta) u_i}$$

where the shape functions are:

$$N_1 = \frac{1}{4}(1 - \xi)(1 - \eta)$$
$$N_2 = \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi)(1 - \eta)$$
$$N_3 = \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi)(1 + \eta)$$
$$N_4 = \frac{1}{4}(1 - \xi)(1 + \eta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4 = 1$ (partition of unity) — a fundamental property all valid shape functions must satisfy.

### 3.2 Derivation: Jacobian for a 4-Node Quadrilateral

Given the same shape functions $N_i(\xi, \eta)$, the global coordinates are:

$$x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta) x_i, \quad y(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta) y_i$$

**Computing partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi} = -\frac{1}{4}(1 - \eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \eta} = -\frac{1}{4}(1 - \xi)$$
$$\frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \xi} = +\frac{1}{4}(1 - \eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \eta} = -\frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi)$$
$$\frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \xi} = +\frac{1}{4}(1 + \eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \eta} = +\frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi)$$
$$\frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \xi} = -\frac{1}{4}(1 + \eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \eta} = +\frac{1}{4}(1 - \xi)$$

**The Jacobian matrix becomes:**

$$[\mathbf{J}] = \begin{bmatrix} \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} x_i & \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} y_i \\ \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} x_i & \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} y_i \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Element Type Comparison

| Property | 2-Node Line | 3-Node Triangle | 4-Node Quad | 8-Node Quad |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|
| Natural coords | $\xi$ | $L_1, L_2, L_3$ | $\xi, \eta$ | $\xi, \eta$ |
| Coordinate range | $-1$ to $+1$ | $0$ to $1$, $\sum L_i = 1$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| DOFs per node | 1 (or 2 for 2D) | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Total DOFs (2D) | 2 | 6 | 8 | 16 |
| Polynomial order | Linear | Linear | Bilinear | Quadratic (incomplete) |
| Gauss points needed (full int.) | 2 | 1 (centroid) | 4 (2x2) | 9 (3x3) |
| Jacobian size | $1 \times 1$ | $2 \times 2$ | $2 \times 2$ | $2 \times 2$ |
| Typical element | Truss, beam | CST plate | Plane stress | Curved boundaries |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Elements in Natural Coordinates

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the hierarchical concept of parent elements used in FEM, mapped from global to natural coordinates.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Global Physical Domain X Y Z] --> B[Element Discretization]
    B --> C1[Master Element 2 Node Line]
    B --> C2[Master Element 3 Node Triangle]
    B --> C3[Master Element 4 Node Quadrilateral]
    B --> C4[Master Element 8 Node Serendipity]

    C1 --> D1[Natural Coordinate XI Range -1 to 1]
    C2 --> D2[Natural Coordinate L1 L2 L3 Area Ratios]
    C3 --> D3[Natural Coordinates XI ETA Range -1 to 1]
    C4 --> D4[Natural Coordinates XI ETA Range -1 to 1]

    D1 --> E1[Jacobian J 1x1 for 1D]
    D2 --> E2[Jacobian J 2x2 Constant for CST]
    D3 --> E3[Jacobian J 2x2 Function of XI ETA]
    D4 --> E4[Jacobian J 2x2 Function of XI ETA]

    E1 --> F1[Gauss Quadrature 2 Point]
    E2 --> F2[Gauss Quadrature 1 Point Centroid]
    E3 --> F3[Gauss Quadrature 2x2 Four Point]
    E4 --> F4[Gauss Quadrature 3x3 Nine Point]

    F1 --> G[Element Stiffness Matrix K]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G

    G --> H[Global Assembly and Solution]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flow: From Physical Element to Computable Integral

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Physical Element Curved Irregular] --> P2[Map to Master Element Square -1 1]
    P2 --> P3[Define Shape Functions in XI ETA]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Jacobian Matrix J]
    P4 --> P5{Is det J positive}
    P5 --> P6[Yes Valid Mapping]
    P5 --> P7[No Distorted Element Reject]
    P6 --> P8[Transform Integrand dA equals det J dXI dETA]
    P8 --> P9[Apply Gauss Quadrature at Sample Points]
    P9 --> P10[Sum Weights times Function Values]
    P10 --> P11[Element K Matrix Numerical Result]
```

### 4.3 Node Numbering Convention for 4-Node Quadrilateral

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[Master Quadrilateral Parent Element] --> N1[Node 1 XI -1 ETA -1]
    Q --> N2[Node 2 XI +1 ETA -1]
    Q --> N3[Node 3 XI +1 ETA +1]
    Q --> N4[Node 4 XI -1 ETA +1]

    N1 --> E12[Edge 1 to 2 Bottom XI varies]
    N2 --> E23[Edge 2 to 3 Right ETA varies]
    N3 --> E34[Edge 3 to 4 Top XI varies]
    N4 --> E41[Edge 4 to 1 Left ETA varies]

    E12 --> CC[Counter Clockwise Convention Required]
    E23 --> CC
    E34 --> CC
    E41 --> CC
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This diagram shows the computational pipeline when using natural coordinates for element matrix formulation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Input Global Node Coordinates xi yi] --> S2[Initialize Shape Function Library NI xi eta]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Partial Derivatives dNI dXI and dNI dETA]
    S3 --> S4[Assemble Jacobian Matrix J]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Determinant of J]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Inverse of J]
    S6 --> S7[Transform Derivatives dNI dx equals J inverse times dNI dXI]
    S7 --> S8[Form B Matrix Strain Displacement]
    S8 --> S9[Form D Matrix Material Constitutive]
    S9 --> S10[Compute Integrand B transpose D B times det J]
    S10 --> S11[Apply Gauss Quadrature Sum wI times F at sample points]
    S11 --> S12[Output Element Stiffness Matrix K]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define the natural coordinate system. Why is it preferred over the global Cartesian coordinate system in the formulation of finite elements?

**Model Answer:**
The natural coordinate system is a dimensionless, element-local coordinate system used to define points within a finite element. Typical natural coordinates include $\xi$ (for line and quadrilateral elements, ranging from $-1$ to $+1$) and area coordinates $L_1, L_2, L_3$ (for triangular elements, where $L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 1$).

It is preferred over the global Cartesian system because:
1. It provides a **bounded, standardized domain** that enables the use of pre-tabulated Gaussian quadrature points.
2. Shape functions become **simple polynomials** in natural coordinates, simplifying differentiation and integration.
3. It allows a **single master element** to be used as a template for any physical element geometry via the Jacobian transformation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
What are area coordinates? For a 3-node triangular element, express the shape functions in terms of these coordinates.

**Model Answer:**
Area coordinates are natural coordinates used for triangular elements. They are defined as the ratio of the area of a sub-triangle to the total area of the element:

$$L_i = \frac{A_i}{A}, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

where $A_i$ is the area of the sub-triangle formed by the interior point and the edge opposite to node $i$, and $A$ is the total element area.

The constraint $L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 1$ must always be satisfied.

The shape functions for a 3-node CST element in terms of area coordinates are simply:

$$N_1 = L_1, \quad N_2 = L_2, \quad N_3 = L_3$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the shape functions $N_1, N_2, N_3, N_4$ for a 4-node bilinear quadrilateral element in natural coordinates $(\xi, \eta)$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For the quadrilateral element with global nodal coordinates as given below, compute the Jacobian matrix $[\mathbf{J}]$ at the centroid $(\xi = 0, \eta = 0)$. (7 Marks)

| Node | $x$ | $y$ |
|------|-----|-----|
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 2 | 0 |
| 3 | 3 | 2 |
| 4 | 0 | 2 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.1 above for the complete derivation. The final shape functions are:

$$N_1 = \frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta), \quad N_2 = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta)$$
$$N_3 = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta), \quad N_4 = \frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)$$

**(b) Step 1: Evaluate Shape Function Derivatives at $\xi = 0, \eta = 0$:**

$$\left.\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi}\right|_{(0,0)} = -\frac{1}{4}(1-0) = -\frac{1}{4}$$
$$\left.\frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \xi}\right|_{(0,0)} = +\frac{1}{4}(1-0) = +\frac{1}{4}$$
$$\left.\frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \xi}\right|_{(0,0)} = +\frac{1}{4}(1+0) = +\frac{1}{4}$$
$$\left.\frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \xi}\right|_{(0,0)} = -\frac{1}{4}(1+0) = -\frac{1}{4}$$

Similarly for $\eta$ derivatives:

$$\left.\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \eta}\right|_{(0,0)} = -\frac{1}{4}(1-0) = -\frac{1}{4}$$
$$\left.\frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \eta}\right|_{(0,0)} = -\frac{1}{4}(1+0) = -\frac{1}{4}$$
$$\left.\frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \eta}\right|_{(0,0)} = +\frac{1}{4}(1+0) = +\frac{1}{4}$$
$$\left.\frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \eta}\right|_{(0,0)} = +\frac{1}{4}(1-0) = +\frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 2: Compute Jacobian Entries**

$$J_{11} = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} x_i$$

Substituting $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 2, x_3 = 3, x_4 = 0$:

$$J_{11} = \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)(0) + \left(+\frac{1}{4}\right)(2) + \left(+\frac{1}{4}\right)(3) + \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)(0)$$

$$J_{11} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{3}{4} + 0 = \frac{5}{4}$$

$$J_{12} = \frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} = \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} y_i$$

Substituting $y_1 = 0, y_2 = 0, y_3 = 2, y_4 = 2$:

$$J_{12} = \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)(0) + \left(+\frac{1}{4}\right)(0) + \left(+\frac{1}{4}\right)(2) + \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)(2)$$

$$J_{12} = 0 + 0 + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} = 0$$

$$J_{21} = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} = \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} x_i$$

$$J_{21} = \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)(0) + \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)(2) + \left(+\frac{1}{4}\right)(3) + \left(+\frac{1}{4}\right)(0)$$

$$J_{21} = 0 - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{3}{4} + 0 = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$J_{22} = \frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} = \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} y_i$$

$$J_{22} = \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)(0) + \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)(0) + \left(+\frac{1}{4}\right)(2) + \left(+\frac{1}{4}\right)(2)$$

$$J_{22} = 0 + 0 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1$$

**Step 3: Final Jacobian Matrix**

$$\boxed{[\mathbf{J}] = \begin{bmatrix} 5/4 & 0 \\ 1/4 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Determinant check:** $\det[\mathbf{J}] = (5/4)(1) - (0)(1/4) = 5/4 > 0$ — mapping is valid.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the Jacobian transformation matrix used in isoparametric element formulation. Discuss the physical significance of $\det[\mathbf{J}]$ and state the conditions for a valid mapping. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 2-node line element has global coordinates $x_1 = 2$ cm and $x_2 = 8$ cm. Express the global coordinate $x$ as a function of the natural coordinate $\xi$. Evaluate the Jacobian $J$ and verify it satisfies the conditions for a valid mapping. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The Jacobian matrix $[\mathbf{J}]$ is the matrix of partial derivatives that maps differential lengths/areas/volumes from natural coordinates to global coordinates.

**Physical Significance of $\det[\mathbf{J}]$:**

In 2D, $\det[\mathbf{J}]$ represents the **ratio of the differential area in global coordinates to the differential area in natural coordinates**:

$$\frac{dA_{\text{global}}}{dA_{\text{natural}}} = \det[\mathbf{J}]$$

**Conditions for a Valid Mapping:**
1. $\det[\mathbf{J}]$ must be **strictly positive** ($\det[\mathbf{J}] > 0$) at every point within the element — this ensures the element orientation is preserved and no "folding" occurs.
2. $\det[\mathbf{J}]$ should be **finite and bounded** (no singularities).
3. The mapping must be **one-to-one** (bijective) within the element domain.
4. For high-order elements, $\det[\mathbf{J}]$ should ideally be **constant** (true for linear elements) to avoid integration errors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** Many students confuse the Jacobian with its inverse. Remember: $[\mathbf{J}]$ transforms from natural to global; $[\mathbf{J}]^{-1}$ transforms from global to natural. Mixing these up yields wrong $[\mathbf{B}]$ matrices and consequently wrong stiffness matrices.

**(b) Step 1: Express $x(\xi)$ Using Shape Functions**

For a 2-node line element:

$$x(\xi) = N_1(\xi) x_1 + N_2(\xi) x_2$$

$$x(\xi) = \left(\frac{1-\xi}{2}\right)(2) + \left(\frac{1+\xi}{2}\right)(8)$$

$$x(\xi) = 1 - \xi + 4 + 4\xi$$

$$\boxed{x(\xi) = 5 + 3\xi}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Jacobian**

For a 1D line element, the Jacobian is simply the derivative:

$$J = \frac{dx}{d\xi} = 3$$

**Step 3: Verify the Mapping**

| Verification Check | Calculation | Result |
|--------------------|-------------|--------|
| At $\xi = -1$ | $x = 5 + 3(-1) = 2$ | Matches $x_1$ |
| At $\xi = +1$ | $x = 5 + 3(+1) = 8$ | Matches $x_2$ |
| Determinant (1D) | $J = 3$ | Positive |
| Length check | $\int_{-1}^{+1} \vert J \vert \, d\xi = 3 \times 2 = 6$ cm | Equals $x_2 - x_1 = 6$ cm |

The Jacobian is constant ($J = 3$), strictly positive, and the mapping is one-to-one. The mapping is **valid**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In the 14-mark question, students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to verify the determinant positivity (1 mark lost).
> 2. Confusing $J$ with $\det[\mathbf{J}]$ in 1D vs 2D contexts (1 mark lost).
> 3. Not showing the substitution $\xi = -1$ and $\xi = +1$ to verify nodal correspondences (2 marks lost).
> 4. Failing to compute the physical length via integration $\int \vert J \vert \, d\xi$ as a sanity check (1 mark lost).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Definition:** Natural coordinates are dimensionless, bounded local coordinates ($\xi, \eta$ or $L_1, L_2, L_3$) used inside a finite element.
- **Range:** $-1 \le \xi \le +1$ for line and quad elements; $0 \le L_i \le 1$ with $\sum L_i = 1$ for triangles.
- **1D Shape Functions:** $N_1 = (1-\xi)/2$, $N_2 = (1+\xi)/2$.
- **4-Node Quad Shape Functions:** $N_i = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi\xi_i)(1+\eta\eta_i)$.
- **CST Shape Functions:** $N_i = L_i$ (simply the area coordinates).
- **Jacobian:** $[\mathbf{J}] = [\partial(x,y)/\partial(\xi,\eta)]$ — the geometric mapping tensor.
- **Physical Meaning:** $\det[\mathbf{J}] = dA_{\text{global}} / dA_{\text{natural}}$.
- **Validity Condition:** $\det[\mathbf{J}] > 0$ everywhere inside the element.
- **Gauss Points (must memorize):** 1D 2-point: $\xi = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$, weights = 1.
- **Gauss Points (must memorize):** 2D 2×2: $\xi, \eta = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$, weights = 1.
- **Key Property:** $\sum N_i = 1$ (partition of unity) — always verify.
- **Element Distortion Warning:** Highly skewed or distorted elements cause $\det[\mathbf{J}]$ to vary drastically — avoid in mesh generation.
- **Master Element Concept:** Always imagine the natural coordinate element as a perfect square/line/triangle, then "stretch" it into the physical shape via $[\mathbf{J}]$.
- **Most Common Exam Trap:** Forgetting the Jacobian determinant when converting $dx \, dy$ to $d\xi \, d\eta$ in element matrix integration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quadrilateral Elements - Serendipity Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET723 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quadrilateral Elements - Serendipity Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Serendipity Elements** are a special family of isoparametric quadrilateral finite elements that were discovered *serendipitously* (by accident) in 1967 at the University College of Swansea by a research group led by Professor Olgierd Zienkiewicz while they were working on a completely different problem. The family includes 4-node, 6-node, 8-node, and higher-order variants, of which the **8-node serendipity quadrilateral (Q8)** is the most widely used in structural and thermal FEM analysis.

The defining characteristic of serendipity elements is:
- Corner nodes are placed at the **four vertices** of the quadrilateral.
- **Mid-side (midside) nodes** are placed at the **midpoint of each edge**.
- **No interior nodes** are used (this is what distinguishes them from Lagrangian quadrilaterals, which include interior nodes).
- Shape functions are constructed as **products of 1-D Lagrange / Hermite-type interpolation polynomials** in the natural coordinate directions $(\xi, \eta)$, where each coordinate ranges from $-1$ to $+1$.

The element is therefore called an **isoparametric element** because the same shape functions used to interpolate the geometry (coordinates) are also used to interpolate the field variable (displacement / temperature / etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you want to estimate the temperature at every point inside a square metal plate that has been heated non-uniformly. You cannot measure everywhere — so you strategically place **thermometers (nodes)** at specific locations.

Think of a flat, slightly distorted quadrilateral plate as a stretched, curved patch on the surface of the Earth.

- A **4-node (Q4) element** is like placing only the four corner posts of a tent and pulling a flat canvas across them — the surface in between is flat and crude.
- A **9-node Lagrangian (Q9)** element is like adding a centre pole *and* four edge ropes *and* four corner ropes — accurate, but heavy to set up.
- An **8-node serendipity (Q8) element** is like the *practical compromise*: the four corner posts, **plus four ropes tied only along the four edges** — no central pole needed. The edge ropes let the canvas sag into a smooth curved surface that fits both the corners *and* the edge midpoints precisely.

This "happy accident" of working beautifully well without an interior node is precisely what gave the family its whimsical name — **serendipity**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants Used

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\xi, \eta$ | Natural (parent) coordinates | $-1 \le \xi, \eta \le +1$ |
| $x, y$ | Global (Cartesian) coordinates | Real numbers (m) |
| $n$ | Total nodes per serendipity element | **8** (most common), 4, 6 |
| $N_i$ | Shape function of node $i$ | $0 \le N_i \le 1$ |
| $\mathbf{J}$ | Jacobian matrix of transformation | $2 \times 2$ for 2-D |
| $t$ | Element thickness | $t > 0$ (m) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Order of the Serendipity Family

The serendipity family is **hierarchically ordered** — lower-order elements are exact subsets of higher-order ones.

| Element | Nodes | Corner Nodes | Mid-Side Nodes | Interior Nodes | Polynomial Degree |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q4 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 0 | Bilinear (1,1) |
| Q6 | 6 | 4 | 2 | 0 | Incomplete quadratic |
| Q8 | **8** | 4 | 4 | 0 | Serendipity quadratic |
| Q12 | 12 | 4 | 8 | 0 | Serendipity cubic |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 ESE, the **8-node Q8** carries the highest weightage. Always master Q8 first.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic of Shape Functions

The shape functions are built by following this **three-stage logic**:

1. **Stage 1 — Single-Coordinate Lagrange Function:**
   Construct the standard 1-D Lagrange polynomial in $\xi$ only (or $\eta$ only) such that it equals **unity at its own node** and **zero at every other node along that edge**.

   $$L_i(\xi) = \prod_{j \ne i} \frac{\xi - \xi_j}{\xi_i - \xi_j}$$

2. **Stage 2 — Multiply with Counterpart:**
   Multiply the $\xi$-Lagrange with the $\eta$-Lagrange so the resulting function equals **1 at node $i$** and **0 at every other node** in both directions simultaneously.

3. **Stage 3 — Apply the "Zero on Opposite Side" Trick:**
   For corner nodes, an additional factor $(\xi\xi_i + \eta\eta_i - 1)$ is appended. This is the *serendipity* factor that **cancels the function on the two opposite edges** — a clever algebraic device to eliminate the need for an interior node while still satisfying the Kronecker-delta property.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise these eight shape functions verbatim. They appear in nearly every KTU question on this module.

| Node $i$ | Type | Natural Coords $(\xi_i, \eta_i)$ | Shape Function $N_i(\xi, \eta)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corner | $(-1, -1)$ | $N_1 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta)(-1-\xi-\eta)$ |
| 2 | Corner | $(+1, -1)$ | $N_2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta)(-1+\xi-\eta)$ |
| 3 | Corner | $(+1, +1)$ | $N_3 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta)(-1+\xi+\eta)$ |
| 4 | Corner | $(-1, +1)$ | $N_4 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)(-1-\xi+\eta)$ |
| 5 | Mid-side ($\xi$-edge) | $(0, -1)$ | $N_5 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1-\xi^{2})(1-\eta)$ |
| 6 | Mid-side ($\xi$-edge) | $(+1, 0)$ | $N_6 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1+\xi)(1-\eta^{2})$ |
| 7 | Mid-side ($\xi$-edge) | $(0, +1)$ | $N_7 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1-\xi^{2})(1+\eta)$ |
| 8 | Mid-side ($\xi$-edge) | $(-1, 0)$ | $N_8 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1-\xi)(1-\eta^{2})$ |

### 2.3.1 Isoparametric Mapping Equations

$$
x(\xi,\eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta)\,x_i, \qquad y(\xi,\eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta)\,y_i
$$

$$
u(\xi,\eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta)\,u_i, \qquad v(\xi,\eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta)\,v_i
$$

### 2.3.2 Jacobian Matrix of Transformation

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} x_i & \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} y_i \\[6pt] \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} x_i & \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} y_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} - \frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta}
$$

### 2.3.3 Global-to-Local Derivative Chain

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial x} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial y} \end{Bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} \end{Bmatrix}, \qquad dA = \det(\mathbf{J})\,d\xi\,d\eta
$$

### 2.3.4 Strain–Displacement (B-matrix) for Plane Problems

$$
\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{u}_e, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial x} & 0 \\[4pt] 0 & \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial y} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial x} \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times 16}
$$

### 2.3.5 Element Stiffness Matrix

$$
\mathbf{K}_e = \int_{-1}^{+1}\int_{-1}^{+1} \mathbf{B}^{\mathrm{T}}\,\mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{B}\;t\,\det(\mathbf{J})\,d\xi\,d\eta
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Curved wing-skin panels (aluminium alloys) — 8-node quad meshes fit curved aerodynamic surfaces with **far fewer elements than Q4** while maintaining accuracy.
- **Civil:** Dam-foundation contact zones — mid-side nodes absorb stress concentrations near re-entrant corners.
- **Mechanical:** Pressure-vessel nozzle reinforcements — quadrilateral elements naturally follow the cylindrical meridian.
- **Thermal:** 2-D heat-conduction in electronic-chip substrates where boundary layers require higher-order interpolation.

## 2.5 Gauss Quadrature Sampling Points for Q8

Because $\det(\mathbf{J})$ and the polynomial products inside $\mathbf{K}_e$ integrate to rational polynomials of degree up to 7 in $\xi$ and $\eta$, a **3 × 3 Gauss rule** is exact. The 2-D product rule uses 1-D points and weights:

| $n$ | Points $\xi_i$ | Weights $w_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | $\pm 0.577350269$ | $1.0$ |
| 3 | $0,\;\pm 0.774596669$ | $0.8888889,\;0.5555556$ |

$$
\mathbf{K}_e = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbf{B}^{\mathrm{T}}(\xi_i,\eta_j)\,\mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{B}(\xi_i,\eta_j)\,t\,\det\!\big(\mathbf{J}(\xi_i,\eta_j)\big)\,w_i w_j
$$

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Corner-Node Shape Function $N_1(\xi,\eta)$

We construct $N_1$ such that:
- $N_1(-1,-1) = 1$
- $N_1 = 0$ at nodes 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8

**Step 1 — Ensure zero on the bottom edge ($\eta = -1$):**
The bottom edge contains nodes 1, 5, 2. So introduce the factor $(1 - \eta)$, which is zero on that entire edge.

**Step 2 — Ensure zero on the left edge ($\xi = -1$):**
The left edge contains nodes 1, 8, 4. So introduce the factor $(1 - \xi)$.

**Step 3 — Introduce the "serendipity correction":**
The product $(1-\xi)(1-\eta)$ already vanishes on those two edges, but it also remains non-zero at nodes 3 (opposite corner) and 6 (right-edge mid-node). To suppress this contribution, multiply by $(\xi\xi_1 + \eta\eta_1 - 1) = (-\xi - \eta - 1) = -(1 + \xi + \eta)$.

**Step 4 — Combine and normalise:**
Now evaluate the *product* at node 1: $(1-\xi)(1-\eta)(-\,(1+\xi+\eta))$ at $(\xi,\eta) = (-1,-1)$:

$$
(1-(-1))(1-(-1))(-(1+(-1)+(-1))) = (2)(2)(-(-1)) = (4)(+1) = 4
$$

Divide by 4 to normalise the function so it equals exactly 1 at node 1:

$$
\boxed{\,N_1(\xi,\eta) = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta)(-1-\xi-\eta)\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Kronecker-delta property at every other node:**

$$
N_1(\xi_2,\eta_2) = N_1(+1,-1) = \tfrac{1}{4}(0)(2)(\dots) = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
N_1(\xi_3,\eta_3) = N_1(+1,+1) = \tfrac{1}{4}(0)(0)(\dots) = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
N_1(\xi_4,\eta_4) = N_1(-1,+1) = \tfrac{1}{4}(2)(0)(\dots) = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
N_1(\xi_5,\eta_5) = N_1(0,-1) = \tfrac{1}{4}(1)(2)(0) = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

The same cancellation works at every other node by construction. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mid-Side Shape Function $N_5(\xi,\eta)$

Node 5 sits at $(\xi,\eta) = (0,-1)$ on the bottom edge.

**Step 1 — Make $N_5$ zero on every edge except the bottom:**
Multiply by $(1 - \eta)$. This kills the function on the bottom edge itself, so we need a factor that *peaks* at $\eta = -1$, namely $(1+\eta)$. Wait — note that the bottom edge corresponds to $\eta = -1$. We need $N_5 = 0$ on the *top* edge ($\eta = +1$) and on the *left* & *right* edges ($\xi = \pm 1$). So:

- Top edge $\Rightarrow$ factor $(1 - \eta^{2})$ since it equals $0$ at $\eta = \pm 1$.
- Left & right edges $\Rightarrow$ factor $(1 - \xi^{2})$ which equals $0$ at $\xi = \pm 1$.

So the natural Lagrange factor is:

$$
N_5 \propto (1-\xi^{2})(1+\eta)
$$

**Step 2 — Normalise at node 5:**
Evaluate at $(0,-1)$:

$$
(1 - 0^{2})(1 + (-1)) = (1)(0) = 0
$$

That is wrong — we have zero at our own node! The fix is to use $(1-\eta)$ instead of $(1+\eta)$ because node 5 is at $\eta = -1$. So:

$$
N_5 \propto (1-\xi^{2})(1-\eta)
$$

Evaluating at $(0,-1)$:

$$
(1 - 0^{2})(1 - (-1)) = (1)(2) = 2
$$

Therefore divide by 2 to normalise:

$$
\boxed{\,N_5(\xi,\eta) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1-\xi^{2})(1-\eta)\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Jacobian Determinant (Analytical)

Differentiate the mapping:

$$
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi} = \tfrac{1}{4}(-1)(1-\eta)(-1-\xi-\eta) + \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta)(-1)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi} = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\eta)\big[-( -1-\xi-\eta) - (1-\xi)\big] = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\eta)(-1+\xi+\eta - 1 + \xi)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi} = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\eta)(2\xi + \eta - 2)
$$

Summing all eight contributions:

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{8}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}x_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} = \sum_{i=1}^{8}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta}x_i
$$

The determinant of the Jacobian is then:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = \left(\sum_{i}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}x_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{j}\frac{\partial N_j}{\partial \eta}y_j\right) - \left(\sum_{i}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}y_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{j}\frac{\partial N_j}{\partial \eta}x_j\right)
$$

For KTU examination, this determinant is computed numerically at each Gauss point and substituted into the stiffness integral.

## 3.4 Complete Stiffness-Matrix Derivation

Starting from the principle of minimum potential energy applied to plane-stress/strain elasticity:

$$
\mathbf{K}_e = \int_{\Omega_e} \mathbf{B}^{\mathrm{T}}\,\mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{B}\,t\,dA
$$

Substitute the isoparametric change of variables:

$$
\mathbf{K}_e = \int_{-1}^{+1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{+1} \mathbf{B}^{\mathrm{T}}(\xi,\eta)\,\mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{B}(\xi,\eta)\,t\,\det\!\big(\mathbf{J}(\xi,\eta)\big)\,d\xi\,d\eta
$$

Apply 2-D Gauss quadrature with $3 \times 3 = 9$ sampling points:

$$
\mathbf{K}_e = \sum_{p=1}^{3}\sum_{q=1}^{3} \mathbf{B}^{\mathrm{T}}(\xi_p,\eta_q)\,\mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{B}(\xi_p,\eta_q)\,t\,\det\!\big(\mathbf{J}(\xi_p,\eta_q)\big)\,w_p\,w_q
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **stiffness matrix is $16 \times 16$** because each of the 8 nodes has 2 degrees of freedom $(u_i, v_i)$. The size $16$ arises as $8 \text{ nodes} \times 2 \text{ DOF/node} = 16$.

## 3.5 Property Verification — Partition of Unity

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{8} N_i(\xi,\eta) = 1 \quad \forall\, (\xi,\eta) \in [-1,+1]^{2}
$$

This can be proved by observing that both the linear parts $(1\pm\xi)(1\pm\eta)$ and the quadratic parts $(1-\xi^{2})(1\pm\eta)$ and $(1\pm\xi)(1-\eta^{2})$ are constructed such that the constant polynomial $f(\xi,\eta) = 1$ is exactly representable, so the interpolant of the constant is the constant itself.

## 3.6 Rigid-Body Translation Test

Setting $u_i = u_0$ (constant) and $v_i = v_0$ (constant) for all nodes:

$$
u(\xi,\eta) = u_0\sum_{i=1}^{8}N_i = u_0 \quad \text{and similarly } v(\xi,\eta) = v_0
$$

Hence the strain $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{u}_e = \mathbf{0}$, which implies $\mathbf{K}_e\,\mathbf{u}_e = \mathbf{0}$. This confirms **no spurious strain energy is generated under rigid-body motion** — a fundamental patch test condition.
